chapters.description: He lands on the image of two wings—fierce appetite and exquisite humility—before closing the talk.

title: Soul Making Dharma (Q & A)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of teachings is from a retreat for experienced practitioners led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee. Although they attempt to outline and elaborate a little on some of the basics of Soulmaking Dharma practice, still the requirements for participation on the retreat included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice; without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: In this Q&A, Rob Burbea clarifies the Soulmaking Dharma vocabulary around soul, Logos, and the create/discover dynamic, while keeping the concepts open rather than rigid. He also connects these ideas to awakening, dukkha, devotion, and the kinds of suffering that can come from soullessness or lack of meaning. The talk moves gradually from definitions into practice implications: how intention, receptivity, and imagination shape what becomes possible, and how energy-body work can be approached with care.
short_summary: Rob Burbea explores Soulmaking Dharma as a flexible, participatory way of understanding perception, practice, and healing.

chapters.title: Opening the Questions
chapters.description: Rob gathers the retreat questions and orients the discussion toward the foundations of Soulmaking Dharma and how it relates to awakening.

chapters.title: What Soul Means
chapters.description: He offers a loose definition of soul as psyche and as the organ of soulful perception, emphasizing openness over fixed terminology.

chapters.title: Create and Discover
chapters.description: Rob explains the create/discover formulation as a useful balance point that names participation without reducing experience to either fabrication or mere finding.

chapters.title: Concepts That Stay Loose
chapters.description: He widens the discussion of Logos and related concepts, stressing that these terms need elasticity if they are to remain fruitful in practice.

chapters.title: Awakening Expands
chapters.description: Rob shows how different people and traditions use 'awakening' in very different ways, from courage before meaninglessness to subtle meditative opening.

chapters.title: Dukkha of Soullessness
chapters.description: He brings the teaching into a broader psychological and cultural frame, including how soulless environments and lack of meaning can be a real form of suffering.

chapters.title: Logos and Devotion
chapters.description: Rob clarifies that even the barest perception involves concept, and he connects imagery, devotion, and love of the path to natural soul-making.

chapters.title: Language and Practice
chapters.description: He argues that naming inner processes helps practitioners see what is already happening, then describes practice as a delicate modulation of effort, grace, and receptivity.

chapters.title: Energy and Caution
chapters.description: Rob answers a final question about the energy body, noting that most people need more harmonized energy rather than less, while acknowledging possible imbalances.

title: Sharing Soul Sense (A Temenos Exercise)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of teachings is from a retreat for experienced practitioners led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee. Although they attempt to outline and elaborate a little on some of the basics of Soulmaking Dharma practice, still the requirements for participation on the retreat included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice; without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea offers a relational exercise for experienced practitioners, beginning with grounding, metta, and simple awareness of the energy body. The practice then moves through sharing in pairs or groups, first around bodily experience, then emotions, and finally something that feels touched by soul. He returns again and again to the sense that speaking, witnessing, and being seen all change the field, and frames the whole exercise as a protected temenos held in kindness.
short_summary: A guided relational temenos exercise in which participants share the energy body, emotions, and soul-touching imagery within a field of metta.

chapters.title: Setting Up the Exercise
chapters.description: Rob introduces the purpose of the practice, explains the optional stages, and has people form groups of three.

chapters.title: Grounding and Metta
chapters.description: He guides a brief settling into posture, grounding, and an initial opening to metta toward self and others.

chapters.title: First Round: Energy Body
chapters.description: Participants begin the first round by expressing what is happening in the energy body while others witness silently.

chapters.title: Witnessing and Response
chapters.description: He clarifies the role of the witness, the lack of commentary, and the importance of tracking how speaking and listening affect the body.

chapters.title: Second Round: Heart and Emotion
chapters.description: The exercise expands to include the heart and emotions, with attention to patterns in what people tend to share or leave out.

chapters.title: Metta as Background
chapters.description: Rob returns to metta as the quiet basis underneath the exchange, and the group continues rotating through the second stage.

chapters.title: Soul Desire and Image
chapters.description: The practice shifts inward to what the heart longs for, what brought each person here, and how devotion or longing may become image.

chapters.title: Soulful Sharing
chapters.description: He introduces the final sharing round, where anything that has touched the soul can be expressed through words, movement, or image within the temenos.

chapters.title: Closing in Reverence
chapters.description: The exercise continues through the last rotations and ends with a return to metta, gratitude, and the sense of being seen and seeing others.

title: The Spreading of Five Wings
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of teachings is from a retreat for experienced practitioners led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee. Although they attempt to outline and elaborate a little on some of the basics of Soulmaking Dharma practice, still the requirements for participation on the retreat included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice; without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea introduces five ways Soulmaking Dharma can widen in range: through the elements of the practice, through self-other-world-eros, through the eros-psyche-logos dynamic, through the spectrum of shadow and light, and through the texture of the energy body. He keeps returning to a simple question: where is the range already alive, and where is it still truncated by habit, fear, or preference? Near the end he adds a subtle point about how the self can become imaginal without needing to appear as a figure in an image.
short_summary: A short framing talk on five domains in which Soulmaking practice can expand in range and depth.

chapters.title: Why Expansion Matters
chapters.description: Burbea frames soulmaking as something organic that tends to widen into new domains and asks what the point of the practice is in terms of opening sacredness.

chapters.title: The Five Domains
chapters.description: He introduces the five possible ranges and begins with the elements of the constellation or lattice, emphasizing how different nodes may feel vivid, cloudy, or still unilluminated.

chapters.title: Self, Other, World
chapters.description: The second range concerns how self, other, and world infuse one another in experience, and he asks whether one of these tends to dominate or remain undeveloped.

chapters.title: Eros and Logos
chapters.description: He adds eros explicitly and then turns to the eros-psyche-logos dynamic, suggesting reflection on how much of the intensity range is available and whether subtle eros is being overlooked.

chapters.title: Dark and Light
chapters.description: The talk widens to the spectrum from darkness, violence, and confusion to what seems pure, light, and angelic, with attention to personal tendency and openness.

chapters.title: Energy Body Texture
chapters.description: Burbea closes the list with the energy body, from luminous and gossamer to dense and solid, inviting listeners to notice what feels natural and what may have been closed off.

chapters.title: Imaginal Self
chapters.description: He ends with a clarification that the self can become imaginal without appearing as an image-figure, and that images may ask something of us in life and practice.

title: Sailing the Oceans of Soul (Q & A)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of teachings is from a retreat for experienced practitioners led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee. Although they attempt to outline and elaborate a little on some of the basics of Soulmaking Dharma practice, still the requirements for participation on the retreat included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice; without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: In this Q&A, Rob Burbea responds to questions about how imaginal practice can be held without collapsing into emptiness, and how emptiness practice can be softened so images do not simply fade away. He moves through practical ways of working with attention, eros, discernment, movement, sound, and the energy body, while keeping the focus on how images become more alive, more subtle, or more universal. The talk also briefly touches on the Heart Sutra, jhana, Kuan Yin, and Jean-Luc Marion’s language of icon and idol as ways of clarifying the imaginal.
short_summary: A Q&A on balancing emptiness and imaginal practice, with practical guidance on eros, discernment, movement, sound, and the energy body.

chapters.title: Heart Sutra Reframed
chapters.description: He uses the Heart Sutra phrase about form and emptiness, substituting imaginal for emptiness to keep an image vivid without letting it dissolve completely.

chapters.title: Why Images Fade
chapters.description: He explains fading through dependent origination, reification, and clinging, showing why images lose solidity when their conditions are seen through.

chapters.title: Modulating the Fading
chapters.description: He offers ways to notice what in the lens is causing fading, and suggests easing off that habit rather than forcing the image to stay fixed.

chapters.title: Eros Restores Form
chapters.description: He describes eros as desire and attraction that can intensify the image again and increase its complexity.

chapters.title: Particulars and Fluidity
chapters.description: He turns to the soulmaking value of particulars, showing how attention to elegance, movement, and subtle qualities can enrich an image.

chapters.title: Form and Emptiness Edge
chapters.description: He frames tantric practice as learning to hold the middle ground where form remains translucent, then widens the frame through jhana and the Buddha’s classical absorptions.

chapters.title: Universal Figures
chapters.description: Using Kuan Yin and related examples, he shows how a figure can become more universal while still retaining a felt form or quality.

chapters.title: Discernment Deepens Practice
chapters.description: He argues that naming, differentiation, and discernment are part of the practice itself, especially when states are subtle or difficult to put into words.

chapters.title: Movement and Stillness
chapters.description: He answers questions about movement, explaining how movement can become image, how image can attach to movement, and how stillness often preserves sensitivity more easily.

title: Wisdom and Desire
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of teachings is from a retreat for experienced practitioners led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee. Although they attempt to outline and elaborate a little on some of the basics of Soulmaking Dharma practice, still the requirements for participation on the retreat included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice; without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea explores desire as a central force in shaping self, life, and world, and asks whether Dharma language has made that force too simple. He contrasts craving with eros, then develops a more nuanced picture in which imaginal perception, subtle letting go, and embodied practice can open desire into beauty, mystery, and sacredness. The talk moves from critique of reductionist views to a practical map of how desire can be worked with skillfully, without pretending it can be mastered completely.
short_summary: A talk on how desire can be understood not only as craving to be reduced, but as a force that can open into eros, imaginal richness, and sacredness.

chapters.title: Desire Shapes Everything
chapters.description: He opens by arguing that our relationship to desire shapes our being, our life, and the worlds we inhabit.

chapters.title: Beyond Simple Renunciation
chapters.description: He questions the idea that the path is simply about getting rid of desire and asks what freedom we actually have with it.

chapters.title: A Wider Psychology
chapters.description: He critiques a narrow Buddhist framing of desire, then turns to the attraction of emptiness as beauty, mystery, and sacredness rather than only suffering reduction.

chapters.title: Practice and Soulmaking
chapters.description: He points to the imaginal dimension already present in practice, names earlier teachings on desire, and introduces a meditative way of working with desire's energy.

chapters.title: Craving Into Eros
chapters.description: He draws the core distinction between craving and eros and describes how skill, mistake, and embodied attention can open one into the other.

chapters.title: From Flatness to Imaginal Depth
chapters.description: Using a narrow definition of eros as a starting point, he shows how desire becomes trapped in horizontal wanting unless imaginal perception and richer ideas are allowed.

chapters.title: Dependent Arising of Desire
chapters.description: He explains how desire, body, image, and view condition each other, and offers practical ways to widen energy and intention so craving can open.

chapters.title: Sacredness Beyond Objects
chapters.description: He reframes what is really wanted beneath object-desire, pointing toward soulmaking, grace, humility, and an ever-widening sense of sacredness.

chapters.title: Closing Distinction
chapters.description: He closes by returning to the need for discernment, skills, and differentiated language if certain gates of practice are to open.

title: Sensitivity, Receptivity, and Respect in Soul Work (Q & A)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of teachings is from a retreat for experienced practitioners led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee. Although they attempt to outline and elaborate a little on some of the basics of Soulmaking Dharma practice, still the requirements for participation on the retreat included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice; without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: In this Q&A, Rob Burbea explores how imaginal practice can be understood through the difference between narrative images and more timeless, iconic ones. He then turns to autonomy, receptivity, and respect: how not to force images, how to work with disappointment when intensity fades, and how eros and inspiration can deepen practice over time. The discussion widens into long-term devotion, the way life itself can become imaginal, and a reflective play with causality and participation.
short_summary: Rob Burbea discusses how to relate to imaginal images with sensitivity, receptivity, and respect, emphasizing autonomy, eros, and the difference between intensity and depth.

chapters.title: Narrative and iconic images
chapters.description: He defines the difference between images that unfold in time and images that feel timeless, and invites listeners to notice how each affects self-sense and practice.

chapters.title: The Dartmoor example
chapters.description: Using a brief image from a walk on Dartmoor, he shows how the mind can add a story that the image itself does not contain.

chapters.title: The concertina field
chapters.description: He describes a field of available images around a person or situation, like a concertina, and explains how one image may be selected without excluding the others.

chapters.title: Intensity and jhāna
chapters.description: He responds to disappointment after a powerful opening, warning against equating intensity with truth or transformation, and comparing imaginal work to the subtleties that emerge after initial jhānic breakthroughs.

chapters.title: Experiment without judgment
chapters.description: He encourages a playful, experimental stance toward images, learning by trying things and noticing what feels more soulmaking rather than treating the process as a matter of right and wrong.

chapters.title: Eros and inspiration
chapters.description: He connects eros with inspiration and artistic work, describing how desire spreads through self and world and can make even the practitioner become an image.

chapters.title: Devotion through difficulty
chapters.description: He reflects on long-term projects such as writing a book, showing how perseverance, dryness, and difficulty can also become imaginal objects.

chapters.title: Life seen from beyond death
chapters.description: He suggests looking back on a life from beyond death, so periods of aridity and devotion can be seen as part of a larger pilgrimage.

chapters.title: Autonomy and causality
chapters.description: He closes by revisiting the autonomy of images, the way they can be both me and not me, and a poetic reversal in which life may express images rather than only being caused by the past.

title: Voice, Movement, and the Possibilities of Soul (2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of teachings is from a retreat for experienced practitioners led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee. Although they attempt to outline and elaborate a little on some of the basics of Soulmaking Dharma practice, still the requirements for participation on the retreat included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice; without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: This talk is an extended set of movement and imaginal practice instructions, beginning with a practical choice about whether to stay in silence or keep exploring movement. Rob then leads a sequence of experiments with stomping, warrior-like posture, vocal hiss, subtle head movement, and pelvic rotation, each time asking what is helpful and what becomes perceptible in the body, emotions, energy, and image. Later he widens the frame to include the room, the earth, the stars, and finally a more explicit imaginal practice shaped by Catherine McGee’s framing of "sensing the soul."
short_summary: Rob Burbea leads a series of optional movement and imaginal practices, moving from grounded bodily sensation into increasingly wide relationship with space, image, and presence.

chapters.title: Choosing the practice
chapters.description: Rob opens by asking each person to notice what would actually be helpful now, whether silence and stillness or more work with movement.

chapters.title: Rooted movement
chapters.description: The group begins with contact, uprightness, and loosening movement, with an emphasis on sensing the body as a field of awareness.

chapters.title: Stomping and awareness
chapters.description: Rob introduces a vigorous stomping practice, first letting movement lead and then noticing how sensation, emotion, imagery, and energy respond.

chapters.title: Warrior in space
chapters.description: A more deliberate warrior image is explored, with the body facing the wall and the practice opening to the room as part of the movement.

chapters.title: Reciprocal space
chapters.description: The instruction deepens into a sense of intimate relationship between movement and the hall, with awareness of rhythm, sensitivity, and the broader space around the body.

chapters.title: Wider horizons
chapters.description: Rob invites practitioners to widen from the room to the world, the earth, and even the cosmos, treating movement as a relationship with something larger.

chapters.title: Voice and subtle motion
chapters.description: The practice shifts to a throat hiss and then to movement in response to voice, followed by a very small head movement meant to refine awareness.

chapters.title: Earth and stars
chapters.description: The head movement is placed between the molten core of the earth and the stars, then the body is explored as part of mysterious manifestation in the cosmos.

chapters.title: Imaginal relationship
chapters.description: After clearing the room, Rob ends with a quieter seated practice that calls up an image or presence and explores whether stillness or movement gives the fuller experience in relation to it.

title: Towards the Imaginal
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of teachings is from a retreat for experienced practitioners led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee. Although they attempt to outline and elaborate a little on some of the basics of Soulmaking Dharma practice, still the requirements for participation on the retreat included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice; without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.

title: Trust, Ethics, Image (Q & A)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of teachings is from a retreat for experienced practitioners led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee. Although they attempt to outline and elaborate a little on some of the basics of Soulmaking Dharma practice, still the requirements for participation on the retreat included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice; without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: In this Q&A, Rob Burbea отвечает on how to work with aggression and sexual eros without simply repressing them or acting them out. He frames the issue through Soulmaking Dharma, emphasizing healthy doubt, patience, trust, and the gradual opening of imaginal space so difficult energies can be held with more care and less pressure. The talk moves from the question of safety and ethics into examples from his own practice, then into the middle way of the imaginal: neither real nor not real, me and not me.
short_summary: Rob Burbea explores how aggression and eros can be met with care, trust, and imaginal practice rather than repression or enactment.

chapters.title: The question of aggression
chapters.description: A practitioner asks how to work with violent impulses, metta, and uncertainty about what is real or controllable.

chapters.title: Healthy doubt
chapters.description: Rob reassures that concern and discrimination are signs of health, and places the question within the ethical seriousness of practice.

chapters.title: Beyond repression
chapters.description: He explains how aggression and eros may become more imaginal and less pressured, rather than being flattened into action or simple inhibition.

chapters.title: Trusting the imaginal
chapters.description: Rob describes the slow development of trust, the role of body-sense over mental suspicion, and the need to give an image a limited field of attention.

chapters.title: Warrior and seduction
chapters.description: Using examples from his own practice, he shows how aggressive and erotic images can become holy, mysterious, and ethically contained.

chapters.title: A question of navigation
chapters.description: A second question asks how to work with an image that feels beautiful but not fully alive, and Rob warns against pressing too hard.

chapters.title: Grace and receptivity
chapters.description: He speaks about grace, resonance, and letting an image work on the heart and energy body rather than trying to force more from it.

chapters.title: Images and ethics
chapters.description: Rob addresses fears that imaginal work may conflict with personal ethics or strengthen unwanted energies outside practice.

chapters.title: Middle way of images
chapters.description: He closes by describing the imaginal as both me and not me, real and not real, and as a path toward freedom, sacredness, and a less ego-centered practice.

title: Voice, Movement, and the Possibilities of Soul (1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of teachings is from a retreat for experienced practitioners led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee. Although they attempt to outline and elaborate a little on some of the basics of Soulmaking Dharma practice, still the requirements for participation on the retreat included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice; without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea explores how movement, gesture, voice, and relationship can become part of Soulmaking practice. The talk moves from a framework for sensing movement as soul, through several playful embodied exercises, into pair work where witnessing, attention, and metta are used to notice how body and psyche shape each other. It is quiet and practical in tone, with repeated emphasis on sensitivity, consent, and doing only what is workable for the body.
short_summary: A practical Soulmaking Dharma talk on movement, voice, and relationship as gateways into sensing experience as soul.

chapters.title: Opening the exploration
chapters.description: Burbea introduces the topic of movement, mudra, and the idea that image, body, and energy body can all be part of practice.

chapters.title: Why move this way
chapters.description: He explains the intention: to sense everyday movement, gesture, and voice as soul, and to open more channels for soul expression.

chapters.title: Finding your edge
chapters.description: The talk slows into a careful discussion of different levels of practice, from basic mindfulness to energy body and social self-consciousness.

chapters.title: First movement games
chapters.description: The group begins with loosening exercises, a drunken walk, and a warrior-like expression that brings in body, voice, throat, and eyes.

chapters.title: From gross to subtle
chapters.description: Burbea shifts back from the strong form to subtler sensing, showing how body, energy, and imaginal feeling can all be noticed at once.

chapters.title: Small smile and hissing
chapters.description: He demonstrates a very small smile and then a hiss, using tiny movements and vocalization to explore ripples in the whole being.

chapters.title: Pair witnessing
chapters.description: The practice turns to two-person work, where one person moves and the other witnesses, with attention kept on relational effects and comfort.

chapters.title: Balance and metta
chapters.description: The final section develops the balance of attention, includes a period of mutual metta, and closes with mover-and-witnesser rounds and integration.

title: Heart Art and an Introduction to Image
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of teachings is from a retreat for experienced practitioners led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee. Although they attempt to outline and elaborate a little on some of the basics of Soulmaking Dharma practice, still the requirements for participation on the retreat included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice; without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea begins by extending energy-body practice into the work of emotion, distinguishing between easing difficulty, honoring it, and learning from it. He then turns to image, showing how imaginal experience can arise from dukkha, emptiness, memory, dream, or deliberate practice, and how the key measure is not visual clarity but whether the relationship is soul-making. The talk moves through several examples, including a deathbed-style relinquishment scene, a baby monkey on the chest, and an inward experience of being music, to show how suffering can remain present while taking on a different dimension.
short_summary: A wide-ranging talk on working with emotion through energy body, emptiness, and imaginal image, with practical examples of how dukkha can become soul-making.

chapters.title: Two aims for the session
chapters.description: Burbea outlines the talk's two themes: extending energy-body practice in relation to emotion, and beginning a discussion of image.

chapters.title: Samadhi and difficulty
chapters.description: He describes how to rest in pleasant energy, work skillfully with contraction, and then consider a different attitude that simply stays with what is difficult.

chapters.title: Self, story, and healing
chapters.description: Using Catherine McGee's no-self practice as a contrast, he explains that emotions may need self, story, and varying degrees of openness for healing to unfold.

chapters.title: Image meets dukkha
chapters.description: He develops the idea that an emotion can be held by metta or an image, and tells the old king and baby monkey story as an example of dukkha remaining present while another harmony enters.

chapters.title: Holy roots of feeling
chapters.description: Burbea broadens the frame to include sanctifying rage, fierceness, hatred, and frustration, and suggests that allowing emotional energy to move can loosen stuckness without forcing catharsis.

chapters.title: Dynamism and movement
chapters.description: He emphasizes the energy in difficult emotion, the way self-sense can shift as energy moves, and how this differs from a more standard jhana-style allowing.

chapters.title: What image means
chapters.description: The second half defines imaginal experience as a relational, not merely visual, mode marked by beauty, eros, autonomy, humility, and duty, and includes a long personal example where emptiness practice gives way to the sense 'I am this music.'

chapters.title: Not every image is imaginal
chapters.description: He contrasts soul-making image with a ghost story and a deva photo, showing that unusual perceptions are not enough by themselves; what matters is the quality of relationship.

chapters.title: How to invite images
chapters.description: Burbea closes with practical ways to support image: practices that liquefy fabrication, working from emotional energy, deliberately imagining places, and drawing on people, memories, dreams, or taught images.

title: Growing the Energy Body (Q & A)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of teachings is from a retreat for experienced practitioners led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee. Although they attempt to outline and elaborate a little on some of the basics of Soulmaking Dharma practice, still the requirements for participation on the retreat included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice; without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: In this Q&A, Rob Burbea clarifies how the energy body can be approached through simple awareness, supportive qualities like peace or happiness, and the use of image. He then explores the practical question of when to stay with what already works and when to experiment, including the relationship between samadhi, imaginal practice, and soulmaking. Later questions widen the frame to collective practice and the subtle difference between ordinary energy-body work and the larger field of sacred, imaginal sensitivity.
short_summary: Rob Burbea answers questions about how to work with the energy body, when to experiment, and how this practice relates to samadhi, imaginal practice, and collective field.

chapters.title: Opening the inquiry
chapters.description: Rob introduces the Q&A as a space for questions about the energy body and how to practice with it.

chapters.title: Qualities and tuning
chapters.description: He explains how attention can be tuned to qualities like peace, happiness, and rapture, and how this supports samadhi.

chapters.title: Working at the edges
chapters.description: Using Catherine's guidance, he discusses exploring what feels unfamiliar or uncomfortable without discarding useful habits.

chapters.title: Staying or shifting
chapters.description: He answers questions about balancing ease with the places that seem blocked, and distinguishes simple mindfulness from energy-body practice.

chapters.title: The imaginal fork
chapters.description: Rob addresses the relationship between soulmaking and samadhi, distinguishing sacredness, image, and the weighting of attention.

chapters.title: Collective field
chapters.description: The discussion turns to whether an energy body can be shared, and Rob affirms both collective energy-body and collective imaginal experience.

chapters.title: Mysterious resonance
chapters.description: He reflects on subtle, perhaps uncanny forms of shared experience and warns against collapsing the imaginal into the merely paranormal.

chapters.title: Wider sensitivity
chapters.description: In the closing section, Rob distinguishes the older skills of emptiness practice from the broader sensitivity of soulmaking, and encourages trust in the indicators already present.

title: The Energy Body (A Little Bit of What, Why, and How)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of teachings is from a retreat for experienced practitioners led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee. Although they attempt to outline and elaborate a little on some of the basics of Soulmaking Dharma practice, still the requirements for participation on the retreat included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice; without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea introduces the “energy body” as a way of attending to bodily experience as field, texture, vibration, and openness rather than as isolated sensations. He then shows how this sensitivity supports samadhi, metta, insight, and imaginal practice, while also warning against collapsing too quickly into fixed views of what the body or the practice “really” is. The talk moves from a short guided exploration into a wider discussion of why flexibility of perception matters, including a brief contrast with chakras and other systems.
short_summary: A practical introduction to sensing the body as an open field of energy, and how that supports samadhi, metta, insight, and imaginal practice.

chapters.title: Introducing the energy body
chapters.description: Burbea names the topic, explains why he is using the term, and frames it as a practical part of the retreat.

chapters.title: What it feels like
chapters.description: He defines the energy body narrowly as a way of attention that senses the body as vibration, texture, and field.

chapters.title: Posture, space, breath
chapters.description: A brief guided exploration uses posture, spacious awareness, and slow breathing to open the body as a field.

chapters.title: Light, metta, and emotion
chapters.description: He adds imagery of light and loving-kindness, then notes how contractions and emotions also show up in the energy body.

chapters.title: Why it matters
chapters.description: He explains how this way of sensing supports samadhi, metta, insight, and imaginal practice, and helps distinguish fixation from soulmaking.

chapters.title: Stillness and practice forks
chapters.description: Burbea recommends keeping the body still for now, then describes the fork between leaning toward energetic coherence or prioritizing the image.

chapters.title: A wider definition
chapters.description: He broadens the term to include felt sense, conceiving, and image, and argues for a phenomenological approach rather than a realist one.

chapters.title: Intention and range
chapters.description: He closes by stressing that the aim is not a fixed pleasant state, but an open-ended range of perception, intention, and energy.

title: Opening Talk ('To Kiss a Falcon')
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of teachings is from a retreat for experienced practitioners led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee. Although they attempt to outline and elaborate a little on some of the basics of Soulmaking Dharma practice, still the requirements for participation on the retreat included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice; without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea opens this retreat by framing Soulmaking Dharma as a set of foundations: not a new system, but a distillation of skills, attitudes, and understandings already present in Buddhist practice. He then develops the idea of practice as an art shaped by beauty, discernment, and a willingness to question assumptions, including assumptions about truth and reality. A birthday poem, a brief Coltrane reference, and a reflection on Western culture help widen the talk toward belonging, ethics, and what it means to be human.
short_summary: An opening talk that frames Soulmaking Dharma as an art of practice, discernment, and openness to the imaginal.

chapters.title: Setting the Foundations
chapters.description: He introduces the retreat as a condensation of foundations rather than a new teaching and names the skills and attitudes the path asks for.

chapters.title: Questioning Assumptions
chapters.description: He stresses that this approach includes the courage to question inherited ideas, while also respecting pacing, digestion, and individual readiness.

chapters.title: Opening the Field
chapters.description: The talk turns to what soulmaking can open up: beauty, art, human possibility, and a wider sense of where the path may lead.

chapters.title: Art, Truth, and Reality
chapters.description: He unpacks his use of the word art and explains the path’s unusual relationship to truth and reality as something that opens experience rather than fixes doctrine.

chapters.title: The Birthday Poem
chapters.description: A poem about red poppies, falcons, belonging, and secret languages becomes a vivid example of relational, imaginal, and embodied teaching.

chapters.title: Culture and Crisis
chapters.description: He reflects on Western inheritance as both brilliant and brutal, linking the talk to ecological and anthropological crises.

chapters.title: Discernment and Precision
chapters.description: He argues that soulmaking requires discriminations, conceptual care, and a balance between precision and elasticity.

chapters.title: Playing the Practice
chapters.description: He closes by encouraging play, experiment, and first-hand experience, with practice and understanding enriching each other over time.

title: Nothing Happens By Itself
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Freely Given Retreats) The talks and exercises from this 'Tending the Holy Fire' retreat are intended for experienced practitioners who already have a working familiarity with this particular Soulmaking paradigm, as outlined, for example, in the following retreats: 'The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course)'; 'Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception'; and 'Of Hermits and Lovers: The Alchemy of Desire'. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course. Without this practice and knowledge base, the material from this retreat may be confusing and unhelpful.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea begins with the simple refrain that nothing happens by itself, then unfolds how soulmaking depends on conditions: care, attention, and the right vessel. He introduces temenos as a protected sacred space, first in solitude and then in relationship, where images and the sensing of soul can be held and allowed to develop. The talk closes by turning this into an open question: what supports soulfulness, and what gets in the way?
short_summary: Rob Burbea explores how soulmaking depends on conditions, protected space, and careful relationship rather than happening automatically.

chapters.title: Nothing Happens Alone
chapters.description: He opens with the central maxim that soulmaking, like practice, requires participation, care, and supporting conditions.

chapters.title: The Temenos
chapters.description: He introduces temenos as an enclosed sacred vessel where the holy fire can be tended and something can unfold.

chapters.title: Images and Samadhi
chapters.description: He suggests that deep absorption is not always necessary and that even brief imaginal contact can have lasting effect.

chapters.title: Soulmaking in Relationship
chapters.description: He turns from solitary practice to the idea that much soulmaking happens between people, through reciprocal spark and beauty.

chapters.title: Creating the Vessel
chapters.description: He describes the need for formality, timing, and care when sharing images so the field can actually hold them.

chapters.title: Honoring What Is Precious
chapters.description: He emphasizes that images and soulfulness are vulnerable and should be treated with honor rather than carelessly disclosed.

chapters.title: An Open Question
chapters.description: He closes by inviting experimentation and asking what helps or hinders the creation of a vessel for soulmaking.

title: Discernment in Soulmaking (Q & A)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Freely Given Retreats) The talks and exercises from this 'Tending the Holy Fire' retreat are intended for experienced practitioners who already have a working familiarity with this particular Soulmaking paradigm, as outlined, for example, in the following retreats: 'The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course)'; 'Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception'; and 'Of Hermits and Lovers: The Alchemy of Desire'. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course. Without this practice and knowledge base, the material from this retreat may be confusing and unhelpful.
podcast_description: This Q&A explores how Rob Burbea uses “imaginal” in relation to soulmaking, and how that language differs from Hillman, Corbin, and Jung. He then turns to practical questions about the active and receptive ends of image work, the role of eros and sexuality, and why ontology matters when practice begins to shift one’s sense of what is real. Throughout, he returns to a quiet criterion: what matters is not forcing a theory, but whether a way of seeing becomes more soulful, spacious, and alive.
short_summary: Rob Burbea answers questions about imaginal practice, eros, and ontology, emphasizing discernment over dogma and asking what actually deepens soulmaking.

chapters.title: What Is Imaginal?
chapters.description: He begins by clarifying that images should not be required to carry fixed meanings, and that his use of imaginal treats psyche itself as image.

chapters.title: Genealogy of the Term
chapters.description: He situates the word imaginal in Corbin, Hillman, and Jung, then explains that different traditions use the term differently.

chapters.title: Image as Perception
chapters.description: He shifts from terminology to practice, arguing that seeing everything as image or perception can open insight, spaciousness, and freedom.

chapters.title: Soulmaking and Skill
chapters.description: He distinguishes liberation from soulmaking, then names the practical foundations needed for the latter, including bodily awareness, emotional skill, and kindness.

chapters.title: Active and Receptive
chapters.description: He answers a question about the spectrum between making an image happen and receiving it, encouraging flexible effort rather than a fixed style.

chapters.title: Autonomy and Trust
chapters.description: Using Coltrane as an example, he says the point is not how deliberately something arose, but whether it feels soulmaking and can be trusted as such.

chapters.title: Eros and Sexuality
chapters.description: He defines eros as movement toward the beloved and explains why sexuality is one manifestation of it, but not its whole range.

chapters.title: Horizontal Desire
chapters.description: He contrasts craving that stays horizontal with a soulmaking eros that gains dimensionality, space, and more choice.

chapters.title: Ontology and Reality
chapters.description: He closes by discussing emptiness, the unsettled question of reality, and why ontology can be practiced as part of soulmaking rather than as a search for final certainty.

title: The Rhythms of Soulmaking and Elements of the Imaginal (23 - 28)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Freely Given Retreats) The talks and exercises from this 'Tending the Holy Fire' retreat are intended for experienced practitioners who already have a working familiarity with this particular Soulmaking paradigm, as outlined, for example, in the following retreats: 'The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course)'; 'Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception'; and 'Of Hermits and Lovers: The Alchemy of Desire'. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course. Without this practice and knowledge base, the material from this retreat may be confusing and unhelpful.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea reflects on the rhythms of soulmaking: how imaginal practice comes and goes, depends on conditions, and is easily distorted by self-judgment or pressure. He widens the frame by placing imaginal work alongside metta, emptiness, energy-body care, desire, and letting go, then turns to several of the later nodes in the retreat notes, including meaningfulness, echoing, values, and participation. Near the end he uses examples like Aslan, music, and the world of the imaginal to show how an image can carry value beyond its form and how practice can be approached as an adventure rather than a demand.
short_summary: A quiet, wide-ranging talk on the conditional rhythms of soulmaking and the later nodes of imaginal practice.

chapters.title: Opening the frame
chapters.description: He introduces the talk as a brief set of general clarifications and sets up the themes he hopes to cover.

chapters.title: Self-judgment and conditions
chapters.description: He shows how inner criticism forms around whatever quality a community emphasizes, and how soulfulness, like any practice result, depends on conditions.

chapters.title: Pressure and the middle way
chapters.description: Using jhana as a parallel, he warns against forcing soulfulness and points toward a balanced attitude of nourishment without pressure.

chapters.title: The palette of practice
chapters.description: He places imaginal practice within a wider palette that includes energy body work, metta, emptiness, desire, and letting go.

chapters.title: Rhythms and blocks
chapters.description: He describes the stuttering, coming-and-going rhythm of soulmaking and the various walls it can meet, including sudden breakthroughs and gradual loosening.

chapters.title: Fullness of intention
chapters.description: He turns to the 23rd node and explains how intention deepens from self-development toward wanting soulmaking itself, or even doing practice for the divine.

chapters.title: Duty and its flavor
chapters.description: He revisits duty as a loaded concept, showing how it changes when paired with divinity or creation and why it needs careful discernment.

chapters.title: Meaning, echoes, and values
chapters.description: He moves through meaningfulness, infinite echoing, mirrored images, and the way values such as nobility and beauty can be sensed within imaginal practice.

chapters.title: Participation and participation
chapters.description: He closes with the 28th node, participation, stressing that we are not outside observers but participants in the imaginal world, the soul of the world, and the divine eros.

title: A Temenos Exercise (in threes)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Freely Given Retreats) The talks and exercises from this 'Tending the Holy Fire' retreat are intended for experienced practitioners who already have a working familiarity with this particular Soulmaking paradigm, as outlined, for example, in the following retreats: 'The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course)'; 'Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception'; and 'Of Hermits and Lovers: The Alchemy of Desire'. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course. Without this practice and knowledge base, the material from this retreat may be confusing and unhelpful.
podcast_description: This talk offers a structured pair and small-group exercise that begins with grounding in the body, then widens into sensing others, metta, and the shared field created by speaking and listening. It moves gradually from simple presence to a more imaginal and relational way of noticing what happens in the body, heart, mind, and between people. Near the end, Rob links the exercise to a Primordial Cosmic Buddha chant, emphasizing that practice can include the full range of experience, not only what feels pleasant or clear.
short_summary: A guided relational exercise in sensing body, metta, and mutual conditioning, ending with a chant that includes all polarities of experience.

chapters.title: Settling Into Ground
chapters.description: The group is invited to settle and ground in bodily contact, earth, and sitting, with attention to how grounding can be sensed in multiple ways.

chapters.title: Opening the Energy Body
chapters.description: Awareness expands from the grounded body into a larger energy body space, with emphasis on bright, sensitive presence that may contract and reopen.

chapters.title: Sensing Self and Others
chapters.description: With eyes open, the practice turns toward holding one's own presence and the presence of the two companions at once.

chapters.title: Metta in the Circle
chapters.description: Rob introduces goodwill toward the other two, including giving and receiving metta while staying open through the senses.

chapters.title: First Round of Sharing
chapters.description: The exercise is explained: one person speaks briefly about their energy body while the others witness silently and track their own process.

chapters.title: Dependent Origination in Dialogue
chapters.description: Rob highlights how speaking, being seen, and listening alter experience moment by moment, and asks participants to include those effects in awareness.

chapters.title: Second Round: Emotions
chapters.description: The group returns to metta and then expands the sharing to include emotions, while witnesses remain fully present and sensitive.

chapters.title: Reverberation and Soul
chapters.description: After more rounds, the practice includes pauses, echoes, and then a deeper inquiry into what has touched the soul or feels meaningful.

chapters.title: Primordial Buddha Chant
chapters.description: Rob frames the final round and closes with the 'Om Ah Hum' chant, describing it as inclusive of light and dark, suffering and bliss, stillness and movement.

title: Destiny, Autonomy, Love, and Soul (Q & A)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Freely Given Retreats) The talks and exercises from this 'Tending the Holy Fire' retreat are intended for experienced practitioners who already have a working familiarity with this particular Soulmaking paradigm, as outlined, for example, in the following retreats: 'The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course)'; 'Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception'; and 'Of Hermits and Lovers: The Alchemy of Desire'. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course. Without this practice and knowledge base, the material from this retreat may be confusing and unhelpful.
podcast_description: In this Q&A, Rob Burbea explores how duty, destiny, autonomy, and love can all arise within soulmaking rather than standing outside it as fixed facts. He distinguishes between a purely intellectual view and a participatory way of engaging an image, where what seems fated may actually be an offering that becomes meaningful through consent, trust, and attention. The talk then moves into darker and more delicate territory, including evil, fear, mental health, and the role of conceptual framework in shaping how images are experienced. It closes with a quieter exploration of loving and being loved by an image, and how that reciprocity can deepen the sense of personhood and mystery.
short_summary: Rob Burbea examines duty, destiny, and autonomy as participatory aspects of soulmaking, then turns to how love, darkness, and conceptual framework shape imaginal practice.

chapters.title: Opening the Question
chapters.description: The conversation begins with the listener's layered question about duty, destiny, autonomy, emptiness, and how image and action belong together.

chapters.title: Duty and Soulmaking
chapters.description: Burbea explains duty as something that arises within soulmaking, where image, sensitivity, and participation all interweave.

chapters.title: Destiny and Choice
chapters.description: He reframes destiny as an offering that becomes destiny only when it is taken up, while insisting that autonomy remains present throughout.

chapters.title: Subtlety and Dark Images
chapters.description: The talk turns to how imaginal work becomes more delicate over time, including questions about terrifying or dark images and how to discern their meaning.

chapters.title: Conceptual Framework
chapters.description: Burbea discusses logos as part of the soulmaking field, showing how culture, belief, and trust shape what an image can become.

chapters.title: Loving and Being Loved
chapters.description: A second question asks how to recognize being loved by an image, and Burbea describes reciprocity, subtle communication, and the image's own desire.

chapters.title: Personhood of the Image
chapters.description: He closes by describing the image as having its own intelligence and personhood, and by showing how eros and logos can make the imaginal world richer and more alive.

title: Elements of the Imaginal (15 - 18)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Freely Given Retreats) The talks and exercises from this 'Tending the Holy Fire' retreat are intended for experienced practitioners who already have a working familiarity with this particular Soulmaking paradigm, as outlined, for example, in the following retreats: 'The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course)'; 'Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception'; and 'Of Hermits and Lovers: The Alchemy of Desire'. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course. Without this practice and knowledge base, the material from this retreat may be confusing and unhelpful.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea continues a retreat teaching on the imaginal by introducing four more nodes in the lattice: non-reducibility, autonomy, twoness, and theatre. He emphasizes that soul-making is intermittent and should be met with a fluid mix of practices, not forced into one mode all day. The talk then moves into a careful critique of reductionism, a discussion of images as primary and autonomous, and a closing account of the imaginal as a middle way between real and not real.
short_summary: A teaching on four imaginal nodes, with practical guidance on balancing soul-making with other Dharma practices and a closing reflection on the imaginal as theatre.

chapters.title: Opening and context
chapters.description: Burbea begins by situating the evening talk after a full day and sets up the plan to move quickly through four more nodes.

chapters.title: Grace as gift
chapters.description: He returns to Catherine's note on grace to frame soul-sensing as an intermittent gift rather than a stable state one can possess.

chapters.title: Moving between practices
chapters.description: He recommends a wide, responsive palette of practices and warns against overusing imaginal practice alone.

chapters.title: Nodes are interwoven
chapters.description: Burbea explains that the nodes are not linear stages and that receptivity and technique, grace and effort, can work together.

chapters.title: Against reductionism
chapters.description: He develops the 15th node by showing how scientific and psychological reductionism flatten the richness and mystery of imaginal perception.

chapters.title: Images as primary
chapters.description: He offers a stronger imaginal view in which images shape life and perception rather than merely reflecting past causes.

chapters.title: Double autonomy
chapters.description: The 16th node is presented as the autonomy of both the imaginal object and the practitioner, who must freely assent to the relation.

chapters.title: Twoness and eros
chapters.description: He describes the 17th node as preserving distinction and erotic tension rather than collapsing into undifferentiated unity.

chapters.title: Theatre and middle way
chapters.description: The final section turns to the 18th node, where the imaginal has a theatre-like quality: neither simply real nor unreal, but a distinct and liberating mode of truth.

title: Soulmaking Rivers (Q & A)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Freely Given Retreats) The talks and exercises from this 'Tending the Holy Fire' retreat are intended for experienced practitioners who already have a working familiarity with this particular Soulmaking paradigm, as outlined, for example, in the following retreats: 'The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course)'; 'Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception'; and 'Of Hermits and Lovers: The Alchemy of Desire'. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course. Without this practice and knowledge base, the material from this retreat may be confusing and unhelpful.
podcast_description: In this Q&A, Rob Burbea responds to questions about God-language, divine imagery, and why certain words can clamp down on practice when they carry old religious wounds. He then widens the frame into a rigorous inquiry into fabrication, emptiness, and the possibility that imaginal practice, samatha, metta, and healing all belong in one larger field. The talk turns toward temenos, communal image-making, and examples such as Aslan and dissolving skeleton imagery to show how form can fade while particular soul-radiance remains.
short_summary: Rob Burbea explores how God-language, fabrication, emptiness, and imaginal practice meet in one larger Dharma field.

chapters.title: God-language and resistance
chapters.description: He begins with the listener's visceral resistance to words like God, sacred, and divine, and looks for ways the language can be loosened without forcing it.

chapters.title: Mystery, value, and poetry
chapters.description: Burbea reframes divine language as open-ended, uncapturable, and precious, more like poetry pointing toward mystery than fixed doctrine.

chapters.title: What is fabricated?
chapters.description: He turns the question of fabrication into a Buddhist investigation that keeps going beyond papanca, self, meaning, and even godlessness.

chapters.title: Temenos and shared image
chapters.description: The talk moves into temenos as an alchemical container where images can land, resonate, and be shared without becoming a rigid religion.

chapters.title: Open image libraries
chapters.description: Burbea discusses how culture, group context, and the aliveness of another person's imagery can seed an open-ended library of images.

chapters.title: Soul-making and suffering
chapters.description: He suggests that personal and cultural difficulties can be folded into soul-making, changing how life events are held and interpreted.

chapters.title: Paths toward the unfabricated
chapters.description: In response to questions about awakening, he explains how samatha, metta, and imaginal practice can all move toward less fabrication in different ways.

chapters.title: Images fading, essence remaining
chapters.description: The close of the talk uses Aslan and a dissolving skeleton to show how an image can lose its form yet retain its emotional and imaginal essence, before returning to the larger mandala of practice.

title: A Temenos Exercise (in fours)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Freely Given Retreats) The talks and exercises from this 'Tending the Holy Fire' retreat are intended for experienced practitioners who already have a working familiarity with this particular Soulmaking paradigm, as outlined, for example, in the following retreats: 'The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course)'; 'Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception'; and 'Of Hermits and Lovers: The Alchemy of Desire'. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course. Without this practice and knowledge base, the material from this retreat may be confusing and unhelpful.
podcast_description: This talk offers a three-stage group exercise grounded in metta, bodily sensitivity, and imaginal witnessing. It begins by settling into the body and exchanging goodwill, then moves into shared rounds where each person expresses what is present in the energy body or emotions through words, sound, gesture, or movement. Toward the end, Rob widens the frame to the effects of speaking and listening, invites a softer imaginal view of the witnesses, and closes with a Pali blessing for all beings.
short_summary: A three-stage group exercise in metta, expressive sharing, and imaginal witnessing, closing with a blessing for all beings.

chapters.title: Settling and Metta
chapters.description: The talk opens by grounding in the body and introducing the exercise as a shared practice of wishing one another well.

chapters.title: Explaining the Choreography
chapters.description: Rob lays out the timed four-person format, including the bell, the minute turns, and the range of ways to express what is present.

chapters.title: First Round of Sharing
chapters.description: The group begins the first stage, with the emphasis on witnessing, receiving, and noticing whatever arises without needing to get it right.

chapters.title: Second Stage: Emotional Expression
chapters.description: He introduces the second stage, where participants share what is emotionally present and explore how others receive them with fuller sensory attention.

chapters.title: After-Effects and Resonance
chapters.description: After the first rounds, the group pauses to notice reverberations in body, heart, self-image, and perception of others.

chapters.title: Deepening the Witness
chapters.description: The practice continues through more sharing, with attention to the effect of speaking and listening and an invitation to imagine the witnesses more poetically, even as angels.

chapters.title: Return to Goodwill
chapters.description: Rob brings everyone back to the foundation of kindness and metta before widening the blessing outward.

chapters.title: Pali Blessing
chapters.description: The closing chant is explained as a blessing meaning may all beings be happy, offered first to the room and then to all beings everywhere.

title: Transformation in Soul (Q & A)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Freely Given Retreats) The talks and exercises from this 'Tending the Holy Fire' retreat are intended for experienced practitioners who already have a working familiarity with this particular Soulmaking paradigm, as outlined, for example, in the following retreats: 'The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course)'; 'Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception'; and 'Of Hermits and Lovers: The Alchemy of Desire'. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course. Without this practice and knowledge base, the material from this retreat may be confusing and unhelpful.
podcast_description: In this Q&A, Rob Burbea explores how imaginal practice, healing, and emptiness can fit together without reducing any one of them to the others. He contrasts a flattened reading of selflessness with a more dimensional view in which self-images, personality, and difficult emotions can all become part of soulmaking. The talk moves from the question of what is being healed, to the role of anger and grief, and then toward a working definition of soul as something flexible, relational, and not fully capturable by concepts.
short_summary: Rob Burbea discusses how imaginal practice, emptiness, and healing can coexist as different but compatible ways of relating to self, emotion, and soul.

chapters.title: Images, healing, emptiness
chapters.description: A listener asks how imaginal self-images relate to Buddhist emptiness and healing, and Rob begins by framing the whole problem.

chapters.title: What is being healed?
chapters.description: Rob examines whether the work is to deconstruct the self, strengthen it, or open another way of understanding what healing means.

chapters.title: Divine uniqueness
chapters.description: He describes a middle way in which a person's uniqueness, including pain and difficulty, can be sensed as having sacred depth rather than being dismissed.

chapters.title: Multiplicity and freedom
chapters.description: Rob explains how soulmaking can loosen rigidity by revealing many possible modes of self, drawing on James Hillman’s language of psychic plurality.

chapters.title: Emptiness and play
chapters.description: He returns to emptiness as a basis for flexible self-construction and says that self can be shaped in the service of soulfulness as well as dukkha reduction.

chapters.title: All directions are good
chapters.description: Rob reflects on boundaries, self-shoring, and psychological work, stressing that the error is not any one path but being trapped in only one view.

chapters.title: Anger, grief, image
chapters.description: In response to a question about difficult emotions, he shows how grief and anger can open into images, duties, and a more vivid sense of soul.

chapters.title: Sharpness without cruelty
chapters.description: Rob distinguishes forceful or cutting expression from cruelty and describes how anger can carry a wider ethical or archetypal purpose.

chapters.title: What soul means
chapters.description: He closes by offering two ways of understanding soul and by showing how practices can expand or contract soulmaking depending on their limits.

title: Soulful Movement and Elements of the Imaginal (6 - 10)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Freely Given Retreats) The talks and exercises from this 'Tending the Holy Fire' retreat are intended for experienced practitioners who already have a working familiarity with this particular Soulmaking paradigm, as outlined, for example, in the following retreats: 'The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course)'; 'Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception'; and 'Of Hermits and Lovers: The Alchemy of Desire'. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course. Without this practice and knowledge base, the material from this retreat may be confusing and unhelpful.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea explores how soulmaking can unfold not only in stillness and silence, but also through movement, sounding, and subtle shifts in the body. He then turns to a set of imaginal “notes” or aspects—such as trust, dimensionality, divinity, and soft boundaries—showing how noticing or gently activating them can enliven an image and deepen practice. In the final portion he places this imaginal mode alongside more familiar Buddhist framings of emptiness and mindfulness, arguing that understanding fabrication can make room for soulful, skillful imagination.
short_summary: A quiet exploration of how movement, trust, and imaginal perception can deepen soulmaking, and how that approach sits alongside emptiness and mindfulness.

chapters.title: Movement and silence
chapters.description: He opens by contrasting the retreat’s culture of stillness with the possibility of discovering soul through movement, sound, and subtle bodily activity.

chapters.title: Opening practice wider
chapters.description: He asks whether practice can include movement and sounding, especially in forms like yoga, tai chi, or walking meditation, without losing depth.

chapters.title: Seeds, not dogma
chapters.description: He sketches the list of imaginal notes as flexible prompts for discernment and warns against turning them into rigid doctrine.

chapters.title: Trust and activation
chapters.description: He explains how a small amount of trust, or deliberate attention to a note like the energy body, can ignite the whole imaginal field.

chapters.title: Soulmaking and edges
chapters.description: He describes soulmaking as the eros-psyche-logos dynamic and then explores how imaginal perception has soft, elastic boundaries rather than fixed ones.

chapters.title: Dimensionality and divinity
chapters.description: He develops the sense that imaginal perception carries other dimensions, including a sense of divinity and beyondness within the appearance itself.

chapters.title: Three Dharma framings
chapters.description: He compares three broad ways Dharma is taught: transcendence into emptiness, mindfulness of things as they are, and the imaginal engagement with appearances.

chapters.title: Fabrication and freedom
chapters.description: He argues that seeing how experience is fabricated can loosen clinging and even legitimate soulful fabrication rather than rule it out.

chapters.title: Closing quiet
chapters.description: He briefly gathers the talk, invites listeners to sense their own way into these terms, and ends in silence.

title: Trusting in Soulmaking (Q & A)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Freely Given Retreats) The talks and exercises from this 'Tending the Holy Fire' retreat are intended for experienced practitioners who already have a working familiarity with this particular Soulmaking paradigm, as outlined, for example, in the following retreats: 'The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course)'; 'Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception'; and 'Of Hermits and Lovers: The Alchemy of Desire'. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course. Without this practice and knowledge base, the material from this retreat may be confusing and unhelpful.
podcast_description: In this Q&A, Rob Burbea speaks about soulmaking as a broad path that can involve samadhi, difficult emotions, desire, emptiness, the imaginal, and the body all at once. He answers questions about how different entrances into practice can open the energy body, how imaginal experience relates to two-ness and oneness, and why clarity and uncertainty both matter. The talk closes by framing soulmaking as something the soul naturally wants, while still allowing concepts to stay open and fertile.
short_summary: Rob Burbea answers questions about the wide, multi-aspected territory of soulmaking, from the energy body and imaginal two-ness to Buddhist frameworks and the tension between clarity and openness.

chapters.title: Opening the field
chapters.description: Rob frames soulmaking as a wide path that can include the whole of practice, and invites questions from any part of it.

chapters.title: Language and energy
chapters.description: A participant describes how chanting and moving opened the energy body, and Rob explores how language, sound, and movement can become entry points.

chapters.title: What can spark soulmaking
chapters.description: He develops the image of a spark or seed, showing how poetry, concepts, music, and other forms can ignite practice.

chapters.title: Broadening beyond one doorway
chapters.description: Rob says soulmaking can begin through one mode and then spread into other sensitivities without forcing anything.

chapters.title: Two-ness in embodiment
chapters.description: He responds to questions about imaginal embodiment, explaining the movement between identification and two-ness, and its role in relational, even erotic, imaginal experience.

chapters.title: Trusting and refining
chapters.description: He offers the phrase about trusting experience while refining view as a practical way to navigate subtle states.

chapters.title: Emptiness and imagination
chapters.description: A question about form and emptiness leads into a discussion of where soulmaking sits in relation to Buddhadharma and vajrayana-style imagination.

chapters.title: Why it stays open
chapters.description: Rob explains why he avoids overly fixed formulations, then turns to the question of how to speak about soulmaking to others without forcing premature clarity.

chapters.title: Clarity with fuzzy edges
chapters.description: He ends by saying precision is valuable, but concepts need some indeterminacy to remain fertile, and by affirming the soul's instinct for soulmaking.

title: To Sense with Soul
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Freely Given Retreats) The talks and exercises from this 'Tending the Holy Fire' retreat are intended for experienced practitioners who already have a working familiarity with this particular Soulmaking paradigm, as outlined, for example, in the following retreats: 'The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course)'; 'Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception'; and 'Of Hermits and Lovers: The Alchemy of Desire'. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course. Without this practice and knowledge base, the material from this retreat may be confusing and unhelpful.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea offers a brief clarification of what he means by “imaginal” by proposing the phrase “sensing with soul.” He contrasts this with the idea of imaginal experience as merely private inner imagery, and instead points to a way of perceiving ordinary things as filled out with extra dimensionality, richness, and aliveness. The talk then turns to the Buddha Dharma framework of the sixth sense, emptiness, and the way practice can cultivate the very instrument that senses soulfully.
short_summary: A short clarification of “sensing with soul” as a way of perceiving the world with imaginal richness, grounded in emptiness and Dharma language.

chapters.title: Why the Term Matters
chapters.description: He begins by pausing the larger retreat teaching to clarify a word choice and the misunderstanding he wants to avoid.

chapters.title: From Imaginal to Soulful
chapters.description: He explains “sensing with soul” as a near-synonym for imaginal perceiving, not a purely interior fantasy.

chapters.title: The World Filled Out
chapters.description: Using the five senses and the Dharma’s sixth sense, he shows how objects, people, and thought can be sensed with added dimensionality.

chapters.title: What Soul Means
chapters.description: He clarifies that soul is not being used as a fixed entity but as shorthand for soulful experience and texture.

chapters.title: Empty Words, Useful Language
chapters.description: He turns to emptiness to show why words like soul, deity, and divinity can be used provisionally without reification.

chapters.title: Making the Instrument
chapters.description: He closes by describing soulmaking as fabricating soulfulness and building the perceptual capacity to sense that way.

title: Elements of the Imaginal (1 - 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
