chapters.description: Burbea argues that understanding how practice works changes what we choose in meditation and how we regard thoughts, images, and emotions.

chapters.title: Rootedness and earth
chapters.description: He turns to hara practice, earth connection, and the Buddha touching the earth as examples of imaginal rootedness and strength.

chapters.title: Spacious equanimity
chapters.description: The talk contrasts solidity with spacious, insubstantial forms of equanimity that allow things to come and go without entanglement.

chapters.title: Strength, libido, vitality
chapters.description: Burbea discusses images of strength, the role of libido and vitality, and the need to avoid pathologizing a person's natural tendencies.

chapters.title: Dynamic balance
chapters.description: He frames balance as fluid and responsive, then uses the jhanas to show that qualities of stillness and equanimity can be accessed, revisited, and trusted over time.

chapters.title: Range and inquiry
chapters.description: The talk widens into a range of being that includes arousal, stillness, mindfulness, and investigation, while questioning fixed ideals of balance.

title: Wisdom, Art, Balance (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea explores two alchemical sayings as a way of thinking about eros, practice, and the conditions that support a fuller path. He returns again and again to the need for a vessel that is strong and flexible enough to hold intensity, and to the question of how to work with fire without either repressing it or being overwhelmed by it. The talk then widens into preliminaries for eros practice: ethics, emotional and energetic skill, trust, and a more nuanced relation to equanimity, freedom, and duty.
short_summary: A grounded exploration of eros, vessel, and equanimity, with practical attention to ethics, energetics, and the supports needed for deeper practice.

chapters.title: Two alchemical maxims
chapters.description: Burbea introduces two alchemical sayings and frames them as broadly relevant to practice, psychotherapy, and healing.

chapters.title: Eros needs a vessel
chapters.description: He explains why opening to eros requires care, boundaries, and a vessel strong enough to hold the work without acting it out.

chapters.title: Elasticity and shattering
chapters.description: The vessel must be able to stretch, and sometimes break, as development outgrows old forms and structures.

chapters.title: Fire and its dangers
chapters.description: He turns to fire as effort, intensity, and eros itself, stressing both the need for mastery and the inevitability of risk and change.

chapters.title: Dionysus and dismemberment
chapters.description: Drawing on James Hillman and Euripides, he uses Dionysian imagery to show eros as disruptive, archetypal, and not reducible to personal blame.

chapters.title: Preliminaries for practice
chapters.description: Burbea asks what must be in place before deeper eros work: ethics, preliminary disciplines, conceptual framing, sangha, and a willingness to question assumptions.

chapters.title: Ethics and rootedness
chapters.description: He argues that eros practice needs a heartfelt, embodied commitment to ethics, not just obedience to rules or fear of consequences.

chapters.title: Energetics and trust
chapters.description: He adds skill with emotion and energy, then a modest trust that allows experimentation and reveals eros as something potentially wise and meaningful.

chapters.title: Equanimity and duty
chapters.description: The talk closes by rethinking equanimity as balance and spaciousness, then linking freedom, imaginal duty, and a disciplined middle way in relation to what matters.

title: Eros Unfettered (Part 5 - Otherness and the Art of Disappearing)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea continues his exploration of eros as part of soulmaking, contrasting it with older ideas of eros as a drive toward union. He argues that eros preserves otherness even while meditative practice can open real experiences of oneness, and then turns to the question of participation: how images, relationships, and Dharma itself become more alive when seen as co-created rather than fixed. The talk widens into questions of imaginal knowing, sacredness, and what it might mean for the Dharma to become endless rather than narrowly therapeutic or narrowly liberationist.
short_summary: A talk on eros, otherness, participation, and why Dharma may need to be understood as endless.

chapters.title: Eros and otherness
chapters.description: Burbea redefines eros against older union-centered accounts and says it preserves two-ness rather than dissolving it.

chapters.title: Oneness and its limits
chapters.description: He examines longing for oneness, distinguishes temporary meditative unions from permanent dissolution, and questions aversion hidden inside transcendence-seeking.

chapters.title: Meditative dissolution
chapters.description: He describes jhana, brahmavihara, cessation, and tantric becoming as real but temporary openings that do not erase distinction.

chapters.title: Eros keeps tension
chapters.description: The talk develops the idea that erotic tension arises from desire for the other, from the object's increasing richness, and from the fact that more always remains.

chapters.title: The birth of us
chapters.description: Burbea describes how eros can constellate a third term, the 'us,' along with child, project, or shared world as new erotic objects.

chapters.title: Not the art of disappearing
chapters.description: He contrasts soulmaking eros with the art of disappearing, saying that valuable experiences of no-self or emptiness are not the same as eros.

chapters.title: Participation and sacredness
chapters.description: The talk moves into participation: images and eros can be granted sacredness and autonomy, or discovered as such, depending on how they are seen.

chapters.title: Ways of knowing
chapters.description: He broadens the discussion into epistemology, bringing in the mundus imaginalis, spiritual sensation, Sufism, and John Archibald Wheeler's participatory universe.

chapters.title: Endless Dharma
chapters.description: Burbea returns to Plato and the Buddha to suggest that emptiness, imaginal practice, Mahayana vastness, bodhisattva service, and soulmaking can each open an endless Dharma.

title: Eros Unfettered (Part 4)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: In this talk, Rob Burbea explores eros as a force that opens perception, interpretation, and soulmaking rather than simple desire. He moves from Plato and Kabbalistic images of infinite meaning into Buddhist language, then turns to the ways eros can be narrowed by literalism, greed, or reification. The talk closes with a careful account of containment: how boundaries and non-acting can become a temenos, a sacred vessel in which eros deepens into imaginal and spiritual creativity.
short_summary: A talk on eros as an opening into infinite meaning, and on how containment can protect that opening for soulmaking.

chapters.title: Eros Opens Meaning
chapters.description: Burbea introduces eros as a force that can either support or block soulmaking, and frames it as something that opens interpretation, beauty, and depth.

chapters.title: Infinite Interpretation
chapters.description: He brings in Plato, Kabbalah, Sanford Drob, and Moses Chaim Rosato to show how eros can make the world and scripture feel inexhaustibly meaningful.

chapters.title: Buddha Nature Language
chapters.description: He translates the same opening into Buddhist terms, linking eros, divine seeing, conceptual creativity, and emptiness to the jhana of Buddha nature.

chapters.title: When Eros Narrows
chapters.description: Burbea describes how, without an imaginal dimension, eros can collapse into greed or horizontal pursuit, especially when nature is seen one-dimensionally.

chapters.title: Dream of the Door
chapters.description: He tells a dream of a carved door, music, rain forest, and stars to show how an image can carry beauty, reverence, and depth without needing to be fully unpacked.

chapters.title: Relationships And Limits
chapters.description: He applies the same pattern to nature, teacher-student devotion, and music, showing how eros fades when an image or relationship can no longer hold the soul's expansion.

chapters.title: Icon, Idol, Concept
chapters.description: Using Tibetan lineage, Henri Corbin, and Jean-Luc Marion, he distinguishes icon from idol and argues that the real danger is reification and identification, not expansion itself.

chapters.title: Temenos And Containment
chapters.description: Burbea explains how restraint and non-acting can create an alchemical vessel or temenos in which erotic tension is preserved and transformed into soulmaking.

chapters.title: James On Desire
chapters.description: He ends with William James on the extravagance of wants, using the quote to suggest that uneasiness and longing can point beyond present understanding.

title: Eros Unfettered (Part 3)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea continues his exploration of eros, psyche, and logos by showing how soulmaking depends on ways of looking that leave room for depth, mystery, and further unfolding. He contrasts rigid materialist or reductionist views with less-fabricating contemplative perspectives, then turns to examples from sexuality, pornography, relationship, and difficulty to show how perception can either flatten experience or re-enchant it. The talk closes by returning to the practical question of how concepts can be lived, flexibly used, and allowed to open rather than close the world.
short_summary: A detailed teaching on how views shape eros, image, and the depth of lived experience.

chapters.title: Opening the frame
chapters.description: Burbea sets up the question of what is needed for soulmaking and why the ideas have to be revisited and digested in lived practice.

chapters.title: Eros as open-ended
chapters.description: He develops the provisional definition of eros as desire to contact more, emphasizing that touch, opening, and knowing have no fixed endpoint.

chapters.title: Three ways of looking
chapters.description: He lays out three broad modes of perception: reductionist realism, less-fabricating practice, and soulmaking ways of looking.

chapters.title: Mystery and depth
chapters.description: The talk turns to the sense of unfathomability in another person, nature, or an image, and how that mystery itself feeds eros.

chapters.title: Eros and logos
chapters.description: Burbea shows how eros generates more image and more world, while logos and psyche must also be able to open enough to receive that movement.

chapters.title: When view flattens
chapters.description: Using materialism as a clear example, he shows how a narrow concept of reality can prevent imaginal depth and force eros into one dimension.

chapters.title: Pornography and greed
chapters.description: He uses pornography and related patterns as examples of objectification, soullessness, and blocked erotic expansion, while noting that kindness may still be present.

chapters.title: Multidimensional soulmaking
chapters.description: Burbea broadens the frame to show how any shrinking of reality to one level limits soulmaking, and how eros, psyche, and logos function together.

chapters.title: Relationship and re-enchantment
chapters.description: He works through fixed images of self, sex, and marriage, then ends with the possibility of meeting difficulty, even dukkha, in a way that restores imaginal depth.

title: Eros Unfettered (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: In this second talk on eros, Rob Burbea develops the idea that erotic life is not just about a subject wanting an object, but about a larger soul-making movement in which self, other, world, and eros co-arise. He moves from basic distinctions between eros and metta into examples from imaginal practice, sexual and non-sexual desire, and cosmopoiesis, showing how perception can open into beauty, divinity, and a living world. The talk ends by widening that frame further, suggesting that eros can be understood as a cosmogonic force that helps make worlds.
short_summary: Rob Burbea explores eros as an imaginal, world-making force that expands self, other, and experience into a wider field of sacredness.

chapters.title: Setting the Trajectory
chapters.description: He introduces the broader aims of the talk and reviews the thread connecting eros, the imaginal, and soul-making.

chapters.title: Dependent Co-Arising
chapters.description: He frames self, other, world, and eros as a fourfold constellation rather than a simple subject-object pair.

chapters.title: Images That Expand
chapters.description: An earlier example of goddess contact and bodily infusion shows how imaginal practice can deepen self and world.

chapters.title: Eros and Metta
chapters.description: He distinguishes eros from mettā with imagination, especially through eros's auto-erotic and dimensional character.

chapters.title: Desire and Interpretation
chapters.description: Using William Blake and a close look at craving, he shows how a way of looking can make desire harden or open.

chapters.title: Cosmopoiesis Opens
chapters.description: He turns to world-making, including Thomas Aquinas's line about beauty yearning to be seen and loved.

chapters.title: Examples in Practice
chapters.description: Several imaginal and erotic examples show how love can spread into body, matter, nature, and subtle energy.

chapters.title: Samadhi and Sacred Time
chapters.description: He describes the choice between absorption and staying with image, then brings in Corbin's idea of hierophantic time.

chapters.title: Expanding Sacredness
chapters.description: He recaps how eros can widen the sense of divinity, self, other, and world, while keeping the experience image-like rather than fixed.

title: Eros Unfettered (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea explores eros as a soulmaking force, asking why erotic imagery and attraction are so central in imaginal practice. He develops eros as desire for contact with the beloved other, then widens the frame to include beauty, otherness, longing, and the way image, self, and world are co-fabricated. The talk moves from conceptual clarification to long, concrete examples, including subtle and overt erotic images, before returning to the practical question of how trust, guilt, or reduction shape what the image can become.
short_summary: A detailed exploration of eros as the desire that animates imaginal practice, expands perception, and supports soulmaking.

chapters.title: Opening the frame
chapters.description: Rob introduces the talk as a careful unfolding of eros and the soulmaking dynamic, with an emphasis on both theory and practical consequence.

chapters.title: Why eros appears
chapters.description: He explains why eros naturally arises in imaginal practice, linking it to beauty, otherness, and the broader range of images that a practice can hold.

chapters.title: Desire for contact
chapters.description: Eros is reframed as a movement toward contact, intimacy, and touch, with Pothos and Himeros used to describe longing and ongoing movement.

chapters.title: Soul loves soul-making
chapters.description: He develops the mutual fertilizing of eros, psyche, and logos, arguing that the soul wants more soul and that image, idea, self, and world expand together.

chapters.title: Subtle erotic images
chapters.description: A series of gentler examples shows that erotic imagery can be tender, luminous, and non-escalatory, not limited to overt sexuality.

chapters.title: Samadhi and transformation
chapters.description: He describes how movie residue, prison imagery, the tiger, and deeper samadhi shift into a more luminous imaginal scene, showing the link between mind state and image.

chapters.title: The dark goddess rite
chapters.description: A long imaginal narrative on a raft at sea explores ritual, blood, devotion, dismemberment, and the wide range of erotic and sacred image-forms.

chapters.title: Trust or collapse
chapters.description: He closes by showing how trust, guilt, or reduction change the fate of the image and whether the soulmaking process opens or folds.

title: Dilemmas and Delineations: How did we get here?! (Part 5)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea explores Emerson’s “two handles” aphorism as a way of approaching desire, eros, emptiness, imagination, and delineation with care. The talk moves from the power and danger of concepts into a subtler argument: distinctions can mislead, but they can also open practice, refine perception, and support soul-making. He closes by stressing that this is not a closed system, but an open field meant to be extended and revised.
short_summary: An inquiry into how eros, concepts, and delineations can either entangle or open practice, and how to tell the difference.

chapters.title: Two Handles
chapters.description: Emerson’s aphorism is used to frame the whole talk: teachings and experiences can be taken up in more than one way.

chapters.title: Eros As Fire
chapters.description: Rob develops eros as a formidable, non-cutesy force that asks for sensitivity, boldness, and careful experimentation.

chapters.title: Diotima and Meaning
chapters.description: Drawing on Plato’s Diotima, he presents eros as a hermeneutical daemon that mediates between the human and divine, and between what is real and less real.

chapters.title: Limits of Understanding
chapters.description: He argues that eros can never be fully grasped and that its richness expands both the object of desire and our understanding of eros itself.

chapters.title: Dangers of Delineation
chapters.description: The talk turns to the risks of conceptual precision, including self-judgment, over-definition, and the way distinctions can create identity problems.

chapters.title: When Distinctions Help
chapters.description: Rob shows that some distinctions reduce fabrication while others increase it, and that both can sometimes serve insight and soul-making.

chapters.title: An Open Path
chapters.description: He ends by explaining that the teaching is meant to guide practice now while remaining open to additions, tributaries, and future creativity.

title: Dilemmas and Delineations: How did we get here?! (Part 4)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea continues a careful argument for why Dharma needs a richer psychology than simple formulas about craving, pleasure, or self-protection. He distinguishes eros from craving, love, sex, and metta, and suggests that imaginal practice can open perception rather than solidify it. The talk moves from cultural assumptions and the need for direct inquiry into a more detailed account of the eros–psyche–logos dynamic, with examples from illness, pilgrimage, and earlier Dharma teachings.
short_summary: A talk on why eros belongs in a fuller Dharma psychology, and how it can open perception rather than reduce it.

chapters.title: Unfamiliar ideas
chapters.description: Burbea acknowledges that these ideas may feel unfamiliar and explains why they are harder to introduce than culturally familiar blends of Dharma with neuroscience or psychotherapy.

chapters.title: Fantasy and image
chapters.description: He argues that image and fantasy are already present in practice and life, and that a fuller Dharma psychology must be able to recognize them rather than dismiss them as papanca.

chapters.title: Holiday or pilgrimage
chapters.description: Using an oncologist conversation as a contrast, he distinguishes secular ideas of quality of life from a pilgrimage-like movement of soul-making.

chapters.title: Find out for yourself
chapters.description: He returns to the Buddha’s ehipassiko teaching and warns against flattening Dharma into quick conclusions, especially about desire and suffering.

chapters.title: Eros in practice
chapters.description: Burbea says eros is already part of imaginal practice, often intertwined with love, and can sometimes open samadhi or deepen compassion in unexpected ways.

chapters.title: Distinguishing love and eros
chapters.description: He carefully separates eros from love, sex, metta, and attraction, while showing how the terms can overlap without being identical.

chapters.title: Eros and perception
chapters.description: The talk develops the eros–psyche–logos dynamic and links it to dependent origination, showing how clinging solidifies perception while eros can loosen and enrich it.

chapters.title: Small and large eros
chapters.description: He closes by distinguishing a narrower definition of eros from the wider soulmaking constellation it implies, and notes how realism or blockage can turn eros into craving.

title: Dilemmas and Delineations: How did we get here?! (Part 3)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1- 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea opens by placing this talk inside a much larger body of teaching on emptiness, dependent origination, imaginal practice, and re-enchantment, and then uses the first part of the session as a kind of glossary. He moves carefully through phenomenology, ways of looking, fabrication, emptiness, image, soul-making, logos, cosmopoiesis, theophany, divinity, and Buddha nature, showing how these terms relate to one another rather than treating them as fixed doctrines. The talk is especially concerned with how perception is shaped, how images become irreducible and meaningful, and how meditative experience can open into a more participatory and sacred sense of the world.
short_summary: A glossary-style talk on emptiness, imaginal practice, and how ways of looking shape perception, soul-making, and divinity.

chapters.title: Entering the framework
chapters.description: He situates the talk within a wider sequence of teachings and explains why the material needs to be heard in order.

chapters.title: Why these words matter
chapters.description: He reflects on reactions to terms like soul, divinity, and God, and argues for approaching them through experience rather than assumptions.

chapters.title: Phenomenology and looking
chapters.description: He defines the phenomenological method, then introduces ways of looking and fabrication as the basis for the whole framework.

chapters.title: Emptiness and freedom
chapters.description: He shows how emptiness follows from fabrication and how freedom lies in recognizing that there are only ways of looking.

chapters.title: What counts as imaginal
chapters.description: He distinguishes imaginal images from mere fantasy or projection, emphasizing otherness, autonomy, beauty, sacredness, and meaning.

chapters.title: Soul-making and eros
chapters.description: He defines soul-making as any way of being that generates soulfulness, then begins to introduce eros, libido, psyche, and logos as related terms.

chapters.title: The eros-psyche-logos dynamic
chapters.description: He expands on the mutual fertilization of eros, psyche, and logos, showing how concepts shape perception and practice.

chapters.title: Cosmopoiesis and theophany
chapters.description: He describes how imaginal practice can spill over into a transformed sense of the cosmos and how particular things can appear as faces of the divine.

chapters.title: Divinity and Buddha nature
chapters.description: He broadens divinity as a category of mystical quality, then closes by sketching a non-dual view of Buddha nature, Dharmakaya, and cosmic awareness.

title: Dilemmas and Delineations: How did we get here?! (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea begins by situating eros within a larger practice framework, using the hermeneutic circle to explain why this term cannot be understood in isolation. He then offers a working definition of eros as a desire for more contact, intimacy, and knowing, while carefully distinguishing it from sexuality, pleasure-seeking, love, or simple bliss. The talk moves into the felt markers of eros—attraction, tension, juiciness, arousal, delight, and its link with imaginal practice and soulmaking—before ending with a distinction between bliss and the more relational delight of soulmaking.
short_summary: A careful opening definition of eros as a relational, soulmaking force, set within Rob Burbea’s broader Dharma framework.

chapters.title: Why This Delineation
chapters.description: Burbea explains why eros matters on the path and why it belongs within imaginal practice, sacredness, and sexuality rather than being reduced to a side issue.

chapters.title: Definitions and Context
chapters.description: He pauses before defining eros to reflect on the use of words in general and to insist that the definition is serving a practice purpose, not claiming final authority.

chapters.title: Hermeneutic Circle
chapters.description: Using the idea of parts and wholes, he shows how eros, mindfulness, and the Dharma can only be understood in context and through movement between small and large views.

chapters.title: Eros as Desire
chapters.description: He begins a simple working definition: eros is a desire for more contact, more connection, more touch, and more knowing of the erotic object or beloved.

chapters.title: The More and Otherness
chapters.description: Through Pothos and related ideas, he emphasizes eros as an impulse toward more, while also preserving otherness rather than collapsing into fixed union.

chapters.title: Recognizing Eros
chapters.description: Burbea describes how to notice eros in practice through sensitivity to mind states, the energy body, attraction, tension, and different levels of intensity.

chapters.title: Juiciness and Arousal
chapters.description: He turns to libido, moisture, and fruitfulness to point toward the juiciness of eros, then links eros with the imaginal, soulmaking, and a broader dynamism of interest.

chapters.title: Delight and Bliss
chapters.description: The talk closes by distinguishing soulmaking delight from meditative bliss, using jhana as a comparison and returning to the idea that eros enriches and expands experience.

title: Dilemmas and Delineations: How did we get here?! (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea begins by situating the retreat as a careful inquiry into eros, desire, and their place within Dharma practice. He recounts a teachers’ meeting where the term proved surprisingly unclear, then uses that confusion to argue that eros needs a distinct and more precise delineation than desire, craving, or love. The talk moves from Buddhist and psychoanalytic references into a larger frame of soulmaking, sacredness, and a more flexible Dharma psychology that can hold both letting go and the creative force of desire.
short_summary: A quiet, expansive introduction to eros as a distinct and spiritually meaningful dimension of practice, not reducible to craving or to metta.

chapters.title: Setting the Inquiry
chapters.description: The talk opens by placing eros inside a larger retreat arc and explaining why it needs careful definition.

chapters.title: Why Context Matters
chapters.description: Rob frames the discussion within insight meditation, then recalls a broader teaching context that makes the topic harder and more interesting.

chapters.title: Many Meanings of Eros
chapters.description: He reviews how different teachers used eros to mean desire, life, or a counterpart to death, showing how little agreement there was.

chapters.title: Desire on the Path
chapters.description: He argues that desire is not simply a problem in Buddhism, citing the four bases of power and the Buddha’s own intensity of aspiration.

chapters.title: Previous Explorations
chapters.description: Rob points listeners to earlier talks on desire, effort, and the ecology of love, preparing the ground for a more developed account here.

chapters.title: A Monk’s Dilemma
chapters.description: A long email from a young monk in love becomes the central example for why eros cannot be handled by simple renunciation or suppression.

chapters.title: Eros Beyond Sex
chapters.description: He defines eros as broader than sexuality, linked with soulfulness, divinity, cosmopoiesis, and a field of inquiry that can sacralize desire.

chapters.title: Relationship and Practice
chapters.description: The talk turns to romantic love, long-term relationship, and the need to understand how eros lives, weakens, or deepens over time.

chapters.title: Toward a Fuller Psychology
chapters.description: Rob places eros alongside craving, emptiness, and non-fabrication, arguing for a more elaborate Dharma psychology that can open new dimensions of experience.

title: The Way of Non-Clinging (Part 3)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: In this third part of the talk, Rob Burbea returns to non-clinging by asking what the teaching is really for, and whether a simple ideal of “letting go” can hold the full range of human life. He moves through art, family, romantic love, politics, and spiritual practice, arguing that some forms of desire can’t be understood only as craving or delusion. The talk then widens into a distinction between ordinary craving, deep inquiry into emptiness, and the different movement he calls eros, which he describes as soulmaking and as opening a fuller sense of existence.
short_summary: Rob Burbea asks whether non-clinging is enough to account for love, art, and soulmaking, and distinguishes craving from eros and emptiness.

chapters.title: Returning to non-clinging
chapters.description: He briefly recalls Part 2 and reopens the question of what non-clinging means when applied as a practice and a view.

chapters.title: What the ideal leaves out
chapters.description: He questions whether a life without clinging is even the right aim, especially when considering engagement, personhood, art, family, and love.

chapters.title: Concepts and Dharma fantasy
chapters.description: He contrasts a narrow Dharma framework with broader ways of living, and shows how fantasy and image shape our sense of awakening.

chapters.title: Why simple frames fail
chapters.description: He argues that reducing romantic love, sexuality, and other deep commitments to a small set of concepts cannot adequately hold lived experience.

chapters.title: Three directions of inquiry
chapters.description: He distinguishes ordinary sense desire, subtler clinging tied to fabrication, and a third movement he begins to call eros.

chapters.title: What practice is for
chapters.description: He asks what each kind of investigation can reveal, and whether ease, happiness, or stress reduction are really the whole point of practice.

chapters.title: Mindfulness and poetry
chapters.description: He introduces discrepancy-based processing, then tests it against poetry, beauty, music, and the lived force of wanting.

chapters.title: Eros and soulmaking
chapters.description: He defines eros as a movement that opens depth, beauty, meaning, and sacredness, and asks whether a framework serves soulmaking.

chapters.title: Two broad paths
chapters.description: He closes by saying some practitioners need more work with craving and letting go, while others are being called toward eros and a wider, richer practice.

title: The Way of Non-Clinging (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea explores non-clinging as a practical way of looking, not as a simple command to stop wanting. He traces how craving, clinging, self, objects, time, and even attention are fabricated together, and shows how practice can use those connections to lessen fabrication and open toward emptiness. The talk moves from ordinary meditative release into Mahayana questions about perception, appearance, and the limits of “just letting go.”
short_summary: A detailed exploration of clinging, fabrication, and ways of looking as the practical route into emptiness and non-clinging.

chapters.title: Opening the question
chapters.description: Rob frames non-clinging as a deeper question about how suffering is made, and suggests approaching it directly in practice rather than only in moments of obvious craving.

chapters.title: Craving and subtle dukkha
chapters.description: He describes how grasping shows up as bodily and energetic contraction, and how releasing it brings calm, softness, and a more precise sense of dukkha.

chapters.title: Self, world, and fabrication
chapters.description: The talk turns to how craving fabricates self and world together, and how reducing clinging makes appearances lighter, blurrier, and less solid.

chapters.title: Ways of looking
chapters.description: Burbea defines a way of looking as the combined mode of perceiving, relating, and conceiving that shapes experience at every moment.

chapters.title: Spectrum of fabrication
chapters.description: Using dependent origination and papancha, he maps experience as a spectrum from dense obsession to increasingly less fabricated states.

chapters.title: Impermanence and the map
chapters.description: He compares impermanence practice with other views, then sets out the Four Noble Truths and dependent origination as a practical map rather than a final theory.

chapters.title: Empty appearances
chapters.description: A castle-and-tool analogy leads into a sustained argument that appearances are empty, dependent on mind and clinging, and that letting go needs a guiding way of looking.

chapters.title: Perception and Mahayana
chapters.description: The talk enters classical Buddhist puzzles about perception in an arahant or Buddha, and then a Mahayana discussion of emptiness, compassion, and appearance.

chapters.title: Progressive letting go
chapters.description: He concludes by showing how non-clinging develops through subtler forms of clinging, like a ladder or raft, and warns against treating simple letting go as the whole of the Dharma.

title: The Way of Non-Clinging (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) PLEASE NOTE: This series of talks is intended for experienced practitioners who have already developed some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. In particular, it is strongly recommended that before approaching this set you study and work with the material from the following talks and series: The Theatre of Selves (Parts 1 - 3); Approaching the Dharma, Part 1 (Unbinding the World), and Part 2 (Liberating Ways of Looking); the three-part series Questioning Awakening, Buddhism Beyond Modernism, In Praise of Restlessness; Image, Mythos, Dharma (Parts 1 - 3); An Ecology of Love (Parts 1 - 4); The Path of the Imaginal (Longer Course); and Re-enchanting the Cosmos: The Poetry of Perception. Integrating that previous material and also taking the talks in this new set in their intended order will, for most, support a better and fuller understanding of the teachings from this course.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea uses the language of craving, clinging, and non-clinging to question a too-simple view of liberation. He works from the Pali Canon and dependent origination, then shifts into ordinary examples like cigarettes, chocolate cake, pain, and the urge to check a phone, showing how desire, self-view, and suffering get bound together. The talk ends by opening toward a deeper possibility: practice can reveal not just less clinging, but a wider freedom in which the unfabricated and the relationship between appearance and non-appearance become part of the path.
short_summary: A careful introduction to non-clinging that argues the Dharma is deeper and broader than simply letting go.

chapters.title: Opening the context
chapters.description: Burbea explains that this talk is a preface to a later exploration of eros and practice, and sets up the question of what kind of Dharma framework best serves liberation.

chapters.title: Pali terms and fetters
chapters.description: He surveys overlapping Pali words for desire and clinging, using the early texts to show both the usefulness and the limits of fine conceptual distinctions.

chapters.title: Dependent origination map
chapters.description: The talk turns to the twelve links as a practical map for seeing how craving and clinging move into suffering.

chapters.title: Everyday craving examples
chapters.description: Chocolate cake and cigarette craving are used to make the abstract sequence feel immediate and embodied.

chapters.title: Anatomy of clinging
chapters.description: Burbea walks through craving, self-building, and proliferation as processes that tighten experience and reinforce suffering, while recommending actual entry points for practice.

chapters.title: Simple letting go
chapters.description: He considers the familiar instruction to let go of everything, then shows how mindfulness, reflection, and recognizing papanca can support it in limited but real ways.

chapters.title: Working with dukkha
chapters.description: The talk becomes more phenomenological, showing how craving feels in body and mind, including aversion, grasping at pleasure, and the pressure that can be tolerated and released.

chapters.title: Impermanence and its limits
chapters.description: Impermanence is presented as a powerful doorway to letting go, but Burbea says this alone still leaves a narrower view of practice than the Dharma allows.

chapters.title: Beyond simplism
chapters.description: The closing section points toward deeper insight into the unfabricated, emptiness, and the relation between appearances and timelessness, preparing for the next talk in the series.

title: Tending the Flame
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: In this closing talk from retreat, Rob Burbea turns toward what happens after silence ends: how to re-enter ordinary life with care for the heart, the energy body, and the people around us. He emphasizes Sangha, reading, listening, and conversation as ways of keeping practice alive, then broadens into the retreat’s imaginal themes, especially the question of realism, the middle way, and whether a view is helpful. The talk ends by placing imaginal work inside a wider, flexible “buffet” of practices and encouraging listeners to keep the fire, intelligence, and play of practice alive in daily life.
short_summary: A closing retreat talk on transitioning out of silence, nourishing practice through Sangha, and trusting the soul-making value of flexible Dharma views.

chapters.title: Leaving Retreat Gently
chapters.description: Burbea opens by speaking to the transition out of silence and the need to notice what the heart and soul may need after retreat.

chapters.title: Sharing With Care
chapters.description: He reflects on sharing retreat experiences with others, stressing sensitivity, timing, and attention to how one is received.

chapters.title: Sangha Keeps the Fire
chapters.description: The talk turns to the nourishment of Sangha, conversation, reading, and listening as essential support for practice after retreat.

chapters.title: The Imaginal Middle Way
chapters.description: He addresses questions around imaginal practice, realism, and the tension between what is real and not real.

chapters.title: Check the Soul-Making
chapters.description: Rather than fixed doctrine, he suggests testing a view by whether it brings aliveness, freedom, and beauty, while remaining wary of authority.

chapters.title: Practice Beyond Retreat
chapters.description: Burbea argues that these practices can be done in short periods and woven into ordinary life, not only on long retreats.

chapters.title: A Wider Practice Tapestry
chapters.description: He situates imaginal and cosmopoetic work within a larger set of mutually nourishing practices and closes by encouraging play, curiosity, and devotion to what the soul longs for.

title: This Fire, This Longing (Q & A)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: In this Q&A, Rob Burbea explores how imaginal practice lives beyond the cushion: in reflection, writing, creative action, subtle reverence, and the way an image colors ordinary life. He then turns to questions about healing, devotion, longing, and how practice can be held for oneself without losing sight of something larger. The talk moves on to image and idea, the place of artistic striving, and finally to the role of effort, non-striving, and energy body practice in deeper meditation.
short_summary: Rob Burbea answers questions on imaginal practice, healing, devotion, artistic striving, and the balance of effort and non-striving in meditation.

chapters.title: Images Off Retreat
chapters.description: He describes how imaginal figures can be honored through meditation, reflection, writing, creative work, or subtle daily reverence.

chapters.title: Healing and Devotion
chapters.description: He responds to the tension between practicing for oneself and practicing for something larger, and suggests holding both without forcing a dichotomy.

chapters.title: From Mind to Heart
chapters.description: He distinguishes a mental agenda from the heart's yearning and links imaginal healing to a broader re-enchantment of perception.

chapters.title: Image and Idea
chapters.description: He clarifies that image and idea are deeply intertwined, including in insight practice, and that meaningful things often live as both.

chapters.title: Dukkha and Soulmaking
chapters.description: He considers whether suffering can become soulmaking and points to the hidden ideas that shape how dukkha is experienced.

chapters.title: The Artist's Dilemma
chapters.description: He reflects on being an artist and a practitioner at once, including the pull between process and result.

chapters.title: Striving and Result
chapters.description: He uses Natalie Goldberg and his own creative life to question Dharma ideas about effort, stress, and caring about the outcome.

chapters.title: What Is Helpful
chapters.description: He widens the discussion to intention, the danger of subtle self-inflation, and the place of striving and non-striving in different traditions.

chapters.title: Energy Body and Responsibility
chapters.description: He closes by discussing modulating effort in practice, the energy body across the jhanas, and the responsibility we have to the soul and to what feels soulmaking.

title: The Gift and the Artifice of Self (Part 3)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: In this third part of The Gift and the Artifice of Self, Rob Burbea continues exploring how meaningfulness, enchantment, and selfhood are woven together. He moves from psychology and poetry into theology and practice, drawing on Einstein, Jung, Hammarskjöld, Nietzsche, Kabbalah, and Orthodox Christianity to suggest that our ways of seeing help create the world we inhabit. The talk keeps returning to a practical question: how do we translate expansive ideas about soul, divinity, and image into an actual way of looking, living, and loving one’s fate?
short_summary: Rob Burbea examines meaningfulness, re-enchantment, and the way practice can turn concepts of self and divinity into lived perception.

chapters.title: Meaning and the self
chapters.description: Burbea begins by linking enchantment of self to meaningfulness, using Einstein and Jung to frame meaning as central to human well-being.

chapters.title: Images need embodiment
chapters.description: He argues that beauty, sacredness, and imaginal experience only become real when they are integrated through embodiment, duty, and life.

chapters.title: Yes to the more-than-human
chapters.description: Dag Hammarskjöld’s diary and a Latin phrase are used to suggest that meaning can arise from answering something larger than the human.

chapters.title: Many roots, many worlds
chapters.description: The talk turns to Chaim Vital and the idea of multiple roots, then to the Torah as read differently in each world, widening the frame of interpretation.

chapters.title: Loving fate and divinity
chapters.description: Burbea develops Amor Fati, Nietzsche’s eternal recurrence, and the possibility of becoming divine as a way of loving life without flattening its pain.

chapters.title: Seeing truly
chapters.description: He brings in Christian and Buddhist language about fallen perception, authentic seeing, and theosis to show perception itself as transformable.

chapters.title: Creating and shaping God
chapters.description: Kabbalistic themes of prayer, Shekhinah, and tikkun ha-olam lead into a radical suggestion that practice can both manifest and shape the divine.

chapters.title: Levels of self
chapters.description: Burbea gathers Plotinus, Vajrayana, and related traditions to argue for layered dimensions of being and a trainable way of seeing self and others as Buddha or deity.

chapters.title: Infinite soulmaking
chapters.description: He ends by describing how even the smallest act can carry infinite resonance when the conceptual frame allows ongoing soulmaking, beauty, and sacredness.

title: The Gift and the Artifice of Self (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: In this talk, Rob Burbea explores how the self, desire, suffering, and imagination can be re-enchanted rather than reduced to psychology alone. He moves through a careful distinction between emptiness practice and imaginal practice, arguing that both can be skillful ways of looking, and that each can loosen fixed views of self and dukkha. The talk also turns to causality, dreams, and archetypal images, asking whether our lives are shaped only by the past or also by future callings and timeless dimensions.
short_summary: Rob Burbea examines re-enchanting self and suffering through imagination, emptiness, desire, and flexible ways of looking.

chapters.title: Why disenchantment happens
chapters.description: He begins by naming the flattening effects of reductionist views and some spiritual emphases that can narrow the sense of personhood and divinity.

chapters.title: What needs re-enchanting
chapters.description: He asks which layers of self need re-enchanting, and expands the question to include imaginal forms and the whole spectrum of self-fabrication.

chapters.title: Desire, eros, and legitimacy
chapters.description: He turns to emotions, desire, and eros, drawing on Corbin, Boehme, and Gregory of Nyssa to argue that longing can be holy and must be given room.

chapters.title: Mind and emptiness
chapters.description: He looks at mind, imagination, and the continuum with the divine, then contrasts this with anatta as a skillful way of looking rather than the only one.

chapters.title: The dark and the good
chapters.description: Using Jung, he asks about the order of practice: cultivate the good first, then learn how even darker or rejected material may be seen as divine.

chapters.title: Suffering and re-storying
chapters.description: He explores how judgment, blame, and inherited explanations can hold suffering in place, and how new stories can loosen and heal it.

chapters.title: Frames of causality
chapters.description: He widens the discussion to archetypal causality and then to dependent co-arising, distinguishing past causes from present fabrication.

chapters.title: Dreams, future, and imaginal life
chapters.description: He considers dreams and images as forward-moving as well as past-bearing, and draws on Corbin’s angel out ahead to describe imaginal transformation.

chapters.title: Artifice and soul-making
chapters.description: He closes by linking Jung, Nietzsche, Wilde, and metta practice to the idea that some artifice is necessary for healing, creativity, and soul-making.

title: The Gift and the Artifice of Self (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea reflects on how modern Western culture, Buddhist teachings, and psychology can all narrow or confuse our sense of self. He contrasts reductionist views with traditions that hold a deeper sacredness in personhood, then argues for a flexible, imaginal way of understanding self and other that can make room for both freedom and difficulty. The talk moves toward a view in which suffering, personality, and mystery are all part of a fuller soul-making process.
short_summary: A wide-ranging reflection on self, secularism, soulfulness, and why flexible view is central to re-enchanting life.

chapters.title: Cultural confusion
chapters.description: Burbea opens by tracing the mixed cultural inheritance that shapes modern ideas of self, virtue, individualism, and humility.

chapters.title: Empty self, wary culture
chapters.description: He turns to Buddhist notions of no-self and explains how they can be misheard in ways that leave people suspicious of selfhood itself.

chapters.title: Fantasy of origins
chapters.description: He challenges narratives about the pure origins of Buddhism and argues that modern realism and reification are also part of the problem.

chapters.title: Re-enchanting self
chapters.description: Burbea distinguishes different kinds of enchantment and begins to sketch what a re-enchantment of self and other would require.

chapters.title: Secular humanism
chapters.description: Using Charles Taylor and Freud, he describes secular culture as oriented toward purely human flourishing and asks what that leaves out.

chapters.title: What cultures make room for
chapters.description: He contrasts modern Western norms with a Burkina Faso story and then with social critique from Foucault and the Frankfurt School.

chapters.title: Sacred depth of person
chapters.description: Burbea gathers Islamic, Buddhist, and Christian references to suggest that the human being can be seen as far deeper and more sacred than modern flatness allows.

chapters.title: Process and flexibility
chapters.description: He qualifies the view of self as process and argues that dependent origination supports a flexible, non-final self-view.

chapters.title: Dukkha and soul-making
chapters.description: The talk broadens into a soulful frame in which personal events, suffering, and time can become part of soul-making rather than mere problems to escape.

title: The Imaginal and the Middle Way (Q & A)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: In this Q&A, Rob Burbea explores how imaginal practice can be held without sliding into belief or dismissal. He moves from encouragement about practicing in ordinary life, to the distinction between self-oriented practice and practice for something larger, and then into a wider middle way that holds multiple views, archetypes, and callings at once. The later questions turn toward renunciation, sacredness, and the role of darker imaginal material, with Rob emphasizing trust, flexibility, and a broader path that includes cooling, equanimity, and soul-making.
short_summary: Rob Burbea answers questions about imaginal practice, the middle way, renunciation, sacredness, and how to include both light and dark material without reifying it.

chapters.title: Opening the Q&A
chapters.description: Rob begins by setting up the question period and answers the first concern about how to continue imaginal practice when the surrounding culture does not support it.

chapters.title: Support off retreat
chapters.description: He emphasizes nourishment, small openings, and the value of short moments of practice in daily life rather than relying on long continuous retreat conditions.

chapters.title: Practice and intention
chapters.description: In response to a question about intention, he distinguishes practice for self-improvement from practice for God, the Buddha, or the bodhisattva path.

chapters.title: A spectrum of views
chapters.description: Rob expands the frame from humanist service to a more multidimensional view, including deity practice, the cosmic Buddha, and the idea that practice can move through different perspectives over time.

chapters.title: Middle way and wobble
chapters.description: He turns to the middle way between reification and dismissal, saying that apparent razor-edge questions often become more spacious with practice and that wobbling is normal.

chapters.title: Renunciation as imaginal
chapters.description: A question about renunciation leads to a discussion of archetypes, fantasy, and how the monk, artist, or renunciate can remain alive as an image without becoming a rigid life identity.

chapters.title: Permission and practice
chapters.description: Rob stresses the role of permission in retreat, showing how group practice can legitimize hidden sensibilities and make them feel shared rather than private or strange.

chapters.title: Cooling intense experience
chapters.description: He addresses fear around strong experiences by placing them in the context of the wider path, recommending more access to coolness, space, and equanimity alongside bliss and intensity.

chapters.title: Making the sacred
chapters.description: In the discussion of pragmatism, he explains that sacredness often begins with bounded ritual or places before widening into an all-pervasive sense of holiness.

title: Sensing Divinity
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea explores two modes of divinity: a universal sense that can arise through love, silence, awareness, and emptiness, and a more personal, imaginal divinity that retains personhood and particularity. He traces how these modes shape desire, eros, self-perception, and the way practice can re-enchant ordinary life without denying suffering or ethical responsibility. The second half turns into a paired exercise in metta and eye contact, bringing the teaching into direct relational practice.
short_summary: Rob Burbea distinguishes universal and personal divinity, then brings the talk into a metta-based relational exercise.

chapters.title: Enchantment and divinity
chapters.description: Rob introduces the retreat's use of enchantment and divinity, and says he will focus on how these meanings shape the path.

chapters.title: Universal oneness
chapters.description: He describes a universal sense of divinity that can appear as love, awareness, silence, peace, or oneness, often through deep practice.

chapters.title: Desire and equanimity
chapters.description: Using Thomas Merton and Catherine McGee as context, he shows how universal divinity can cool desire and widen the space around life.

chapters.title: Personal and imaginal
chapters.description: He turns to a more personal divinity in and through figures, objects, the self, and the imaginal, emphasizing that the two modes overlap.

chapters.title: Re-enchanting the self
chapters.description: Rob asks what it would mean to know one's own divinity and how emptiness, metta, and imaginal practice can support that seeing.

chapters.title: Perfection and surrender
chapters.description: He reflects on Neem Karoli Baba and Thomas Merton to show how surrender, poetry, and universal perception can reveal a deeper perfection without bypassing suffering.

chapters.title: Buddha nature and tantra
chapters.description: Rob moves through Tantra, Judaism, and Buddha nature to suggest that desire, anger, action, and consciousness itself can be experienced as blessed or divine.

chapters.title: Divine fire in particulars
chapters.description: He settles the teaching into the claim that personhood need not be erased but can be inflamed and perceived as divine in its unique details.

chapters.title: Metta and the gaze
chapters.description: The talk becomes a guided pair practice: giving and receiving metta, then sensing the consciousness and radiance in another person's eyes before closing with blessing and care.

title: Creating the Path (Q & A)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: In this Q&A, Rob Burbea explores how imaginal practice can make the world feel sacred without collapsing into either rigid metaphysics or mere self-projection. He moves from Tara and other forms, to art and dependent arising, to sexuality, eros, and the ways an image can retain autonomy while still being part of practice. The talk closes with a sober reflection on risks, path-specific dangers, and the question of what one most deeply wants from Dharma life.
short_summary: Rob Burbea answers questions about imaginal practice, eros, cosmopoiesis, and the risks and responsibilities of walking a soulful path.

chapters.title: Imaginal forms
chapters.description: Rob weighs different ways of understanding Tara and other divine figures, from an objective imaginal realm to the role of mind and culture in perception.

chapters.title: Art and dependence
chapters.description: He uses Beethoven and art theory to show that aesthetic response arises dependently from the work, the listener, and the surrounding culture.

chapters.title: Practice and sacredness
chapters.description: The discussion turns to how practice changes perception, including seeing Tara in ordinary or even difficult objects, and to a practical way of judging whether something is truly wholesome.

chapters.title: Cosmopoesis
chapters.description: Rob explains how imaginal perception can spill over into the world so that the whole cosmos is seen as light, fire, music, or Tara's presence.

chapters.title: Eros and otherness
chapters.description: He distinguishes oneness experiences from an imaginal mode in which a figure retains character, otherness, and erotic charge without collapsing into merging.

chapters.title: Texture and autonomy
chapters.description: A listener asks about kinesthetic experience, and Rob clarifies that image may be nonvisual, may hold a subtle self-sense, and can feel autonomous without being separate.

chapters.title: Emptiness and fluidity
chapters.description: He brings the teaching back to dependent arising and emptiness, emphasizing that self and image have no inherent existence and can shift fluidly from moment to moment.

chapters.title: Paths and dangers
chapters.description: A question about Theravada and Mahayana leads into a wider reflection on spiritual ambition, fear about outcomes, and the specific dangers of each path, including being swept away or reifying experience.

chapters.title: What matters most
chapters.description: In the closing movement, Rob asks whether freedom is really the deepest value for each person, and invites an honest look at sacredness, beauty, art, and authenticity.

title: Images of the Breath: Energy Body, Colour, and the Beloved (Guided Meditation)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea offers a guided meditation that moves from the felt energy of the body into several imaginal ways of breathing: through different centers, through the whole field, as light, and as color. The practice then opens into blessing and relationship, including the possibility of meeting an imaginal beloved and breathing in harmony with that figure. The talk ends by inviting listeners to notice how image changes the sense of self, others, and world.
short_summary: A guided meditation on breath, energy body, light, color, and imaginal relationship.

chapters.title: Why images matter
chapters.description: Burbea briefly frames imaginal practice as something that develops with familiarity and interest, and notes how it can interweave with samadhi and emptiness practice.

chapters.title: Settling the body
chapters.description: The guided meditation begins by establishing posture, spreading awareness through the body, and sensing the energy field as alive and responsive.

chapters.title: Lower centers and grounding
chapters.description: He explores breathing at the lower belly, perineum, feet, and lower back, inviting attention to what feels comfortable and good.

chapters.title: Heart and crown
chapters.description: The practice expands to the back of the heart and then the crown of the head, always emphasizing that the whole body remains involved.

chapters.title: Front of body opening
chapters.description: Attention moves to the chest and solar plexus, with the breath imagined as opening and softening the body from the front.

chapters.title: Porous field of breath
chapters.description: Burbea invites the sense of breath and energy arriving from all sides or growing from within, and lets the breath become very subtle or still.

chapters.title: Light and color
chapters.description: The breath is then felt as light, given color, and noticed for its different qualities, with green, red, and blue used as examples of distinct felt tones.

chapters.title: Blessing radiance
chapters.description: The light is allowed to radiate outward as healing or blessing, reshaping the surrounding world into a sacred mandala or Buddha realm.

chapters.title: Meeting the beloved
chapters.description: An imaginal figure, perhaps a beloved, is brought into the field and the meditation explores breathing together, passing breath back and forth, and opening to love.

title: From Inertia, Towards the Solutio (A Fragment)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Please Note: This series of talks is from a retreat led by Rob Burbea and Catherine McGee for experienced practitioners. The requirements for participation included some understanding of and working familiarity with practices of emptiness, samatha, metta, the emotional/energy body, and the imaginal, as well as basic mindfulness practice. Without this experience it is possible that the material and teachings from this retreat will be difficult to understand and confusing for some.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea offers a short retreat talk on loosening the mind’s inertia through wider sensory awareness and a more imaginal way of perceiving. He connects the energy body to walking, standing, and the sense of a sacred landscape, suggesting that meditation can include prayerful relation to the world rather than only stillness.
