chapters.description: Burbea brings in Tikkun Ha'olam, Corbin’s imaginal faculty, and Nietzsche to suggest that perception, interpretation, and life practice can be part of a larger healing and completion beyond the self.

chapters.title: Demands of the Imaginal
chapters.description: In the closing section, he names three invitations of imaginal practice: to open to the imaginal realm, to welcome multiplicity, and to recognize that image, myth, and fantasy already saturate ordinary life.

title: The Love and Demands of the Imaginal (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) The set of talks and meditations from this course outlines the foundations and some of the possibilities for opening up a practice of the imaginal. Please note that this set forms a progressively unfolding series of teachings, so the talks and practices will probably be more fully understood and absorbed if they are taken in order.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea explores what he means by an image in imaginal practice: something more alive than metaphor, with its own autonomy, love, and demand. He then works carefully through the middle way between literalism and nihilism, showing how meaning is shaped by a way of looking and why emptiness and liquidity matter here. The talk moves through healing examples, compassion practice, erotic and artistic images, and ends by widening the question of what the self is actually here to serve.
short_summary: A careful opening exploration of imaginal images as living presences that can love, demand, heal, and reshape how practice and life are understood.

chapters.title: Image, not metaphor
chapters.description: He begins by distinguishing image from ordinary metaphor, stressing aliveness, autonomy, and richness.

chapters.title: Love and demand
chapters.description: He opens the themes of love, necessity, and demand, and shows how they arise through a way of looking.

chapters.title: Middle way, not literalism
chapters.description: He places imaginal practice between scientific proof and nihilistic dismissal, comparing it more to art than to propositions.

chapters.title: Healing old wounds
chapters.description: Through a retreatant's story, he shows an image working directly with early trauma and bodily memory.

chapters.title: Authority and holiness
chapters.description: He turns to Thich Nhat Hanh, then to the tramp and Christ, to show how images can carry authority, outcast-ness, and hidden sanctity.

chapters.title: Compassion and eros
chapters.description: A guided compassion practice unexpectedly opens into erotic imaginal experience, revealing how self and image can interpenetrate.

chapters.title: Being stretched
chapters.description: He discusses how imaginal figures can demand a trial, and why liquidity and emptiness are needed to keep the view non-literal.

chapters.title: What is healing?
chapters.description: He pauses on healing, then moves through Aphrodite-like beauty and the gradual widening of sacredness through witnessing.

chapters.title: Sculptor and Coltrane
chapters.description: He uses a master sculptor and John Coltrane as examples of demanding, loving presences that shape creative work.

title: Opening, Tuning, and Relating to the Imaginal: Instructions (3)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) The set of talks and meditations from this course outlines the foundations and some of the possibilities for opening up a practice of the imaginal. Please note that this set forms a progressively unfolding series of teachings, so the talks and practices will probably be more fully understood and absorbed if they are taken in order.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea clarifies how walking meditation can serve either samadhi or imaginal practice, then turns to how images are related to: how long to stay with them, what they seem to want, and how love, eros, and reverence shape the practice. He shares several vivid examples — a lion, phoenix, fox, unicorn, artist’s studio, Quan Yin, and Christ — to show that imaginal figures can heal, instruct, and reframe how life is seen. The talk keeps returning to a simple test: does this feel helpful, nourishing, and soulful?
short_summary: A detailed set of instructions on walking practice, imaginal relationship, and using soulfulness rather than right-or-wrong as the guide.

chapters.title: Walking as Practice
chapters.description: He clarifies that walking is a posture, and shows how it can host either samadhi or imaginal practice through energy-body awareness.

chapters.title: Walking with Images
chapters.description: He gives walking-metta and imaginal examples, then corrects the idea that images should be held for a fixed amount of time.

chapters.title: What the Figure Wants
chapters.description: He reframes the relation to an imaginal figure as asking what it wants, including being seen, honored, or looked at in a particular way.

chapters.title: Healing Imaginal Encounters
chapters.description: Through the lion, phoenix, and fox stories, he shows how images can bring bodily healing, protection, erotic charge, and reverence.

chapters.title: Love and Eros
chapters.description: He explores the particularity of love for imaginal figures, using Eric Dolphy and Mary Oliver to show how love and eros can deepen sacredness.

chapters.title: Images and Life
chapters.description: He shares a story of asking priest-like figures about writing a book on emptiness, then emphasizes that soulmaking matters more than advice.

chapters.title: Literal and Nonliteral
chapters.description: The moonlit lake and unicorn, then the painter's studio, illustrate how images can feel deeply real without being read as literal instructions.

chapters.title: Doubt and Experiment
chapters.description: He addresses doubt, fear of doing it wrong, and the worry of making things up, replacing them with experimentation and a soulfulness check.

chapters.title: Octaves and Portability
chapters.description: He closes by describing the register of an image, how it shifts with mind state, and how imaginal figures can be carried into daily life and wider perceptions.

title: Soulmaking (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) The set of talks and meditations from this course outlines the foundations and some of the possibilities for opening up a practice of the imaginal. Please note that this set forms a progressively unfolding series of teachings, so the talks and practices will probably be more fully understood and absorbed if they are taken in order.
podcast_description: In this second talk on soulmaking, Rob Burbea asks how imaginal practice relates to the Dharma without reducing either one to a fixed doctrine. He distinguishes between ways of looking that fabricate less, ways that dissolve perception more deeply, and ways that deliberately and skillfully fabricate image and self, then explores how that third mode opens room for fantasy, mythos, and soulfulness. The talk moves from the Four Noble Truths and dependent origination toward a more open-ended view in which concepts are used as lenses, images are honored as real forces in practice, and eros helps perception deepen.
short_summary: Rob Burbea explores how imaginal practice, eros, and soulfulness can be understood as part of Dharma practice without collapsing into literal belief.

chapters.title: Ways of Looking
chapters.description: He frames Dharma practice as developing flexible ways of looking and distinguishes approaches that fabricate less from those that unmake experience more deeply.

chapters.title: Three Practice Avenues
chapters.description: Burbea lays out three broad directions: bare attention, deeper unfabricating practices, and skillful fabrication that makes room for imaginal work.

chapters.title: Fantasy in Dharma
chapters.description: He shows how myth, image, and fantasy already shape our sense of Buddha, awakening, and the path, whether or not we notice it.

chapters.title: Self and Emptiness
chapters.description: Using dependent origination, he discusses the self as empty and then argues that this emptiness can allow more room for imaginal play.

chapters.title: Images and Dukkha
chapters.description: He turns to the Four Noble Truths and shows how images of suffering, compassion, and beauty can be used to meet dukkha skillfully.

chapters.title: Soulmaking as Priority
chapters.description: Burbea asks what the soul wants, and suggests that making soulfulness a priority changes the way practice is oriented and understood.

chapters.title: Dharma on Imaginal Ground
chapters.description: He treats disputes about what is 'really Buddhism' as psychological and philosophical questions about the fantasies and assumptions underlying any Dharma framework.

chapters.title: Concepts as Lenses
chapters.description: He reinterprets Dharma concepts as tools for perception rather than realities, and links that view to more flexible, open-ended practice.

chapters.title: Love, Eros, and Opening
chapters.description: He explores how love, eros, and conceptual frameworks feed each other, using Keith Jarrett as an example of image deepened by love and of soulmaking through perception.

title: Soulmaking (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) The set of talks and meditations from this course outlines the foundations and some of the possibilities for opening up a practice of the imaginal. Please note that this set forms a progressively unfolding series of teachings, so the talks and practices will probably be more fully understood and absorbed if they are taken in order.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea introduces soul and soulfulness as active, relational qualities rather than as a fixed inner entity. He distinguishes soulmaking from panic, delusion, and bare attention, then opens a wider map of Dharma as many ways of looking, including skillful fabrication, image, myth, and fantasy. The talk moves through Buddhist, Kabbalistic, Jungian, and mythic references to show how imagination can be empty and still deeply meaningful.
short_summary: A wide-ranging opening to soulmaking as a practice of ways of looking, image, and meaning, rather than a fixed soul-substance.

chapters.title: Opening the term
chapters.description: Burbea explains that he will unpack what soul and soulfulness might mean for practice and Dharma, rather than treating the word as a fixed doctrine.

chapters.title: Soul as making
chapters.description: He shifts from soul as an entity to soulmaking as a verb, using Keats to frame soulfulness as something nourished by ways of looking, relating, and acting.

chapters.title: Soulfulness and papanca
chapters.description: The talk distinguishes soulful image from hallucination and papanca, stressing that soulmaking has spaciousness, beauty, and a recognized sense of image.

chapters.title: Traditions and comparators
chapters.description: Burbea compares his view with Kabbalah, Buddha nature, and Jungian/archetypal language to show how different traditions authorize meaning and interpretation.

chapters.title: Three modes of practice
chapters.description: He defines Dharma practice as many ways of looking, then sketches three broad modes: bare attention, decreasing fabrication, and skillful fabrication.

chapters.title: Culture and modernity
chapters.description: The talk widens into a critique of modern objectivity and measurement, drawing on Tarnas to describe the loss of emotional, aesthetic, ethical, and imaginative reality.

chapters.title: Hospitals and mythos
chapters.description: Using medical settings as an example, Burbea asks what happens when soulfulness is crowded out, then turns to the unavoidable mythic and imaginal frameworks around practice, food, and renunciation.

chapters.title: Emptiness frees imagination
chapters.description: He argues that deep understanding of emptiness frees fabrication rather than opposing it, and that image, fantasy, and conceptual flexibility can be part of practice without literalism.

chapters.title: Mythic life and soul story
chapters.description: In the closing movement, Burbea draws on Jung, Hillman, Homer, Proclus, and Ibn Arabi to suggest that we live within a larger soul story, one that reconnects life with archetype, depth, and the vertical dimension.

title: Opening, Tuning, and Relating to the Imaginal: Instructions (2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) The set of talks and meditations from this course outlines the foundations and some of the possibilities for opening up a practice of the imaginal. Please note that this set forms a progressively unfolding series of teachings, so the talks and practices will probably be more fully understood and absorbed if they are taken in order.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea continues a practical talk on imaginal practice, defining it as mindfulness with images rather than daydreaming. He then lays out the main threads of attention: how an image is sensed, how it may fill out, how its character and specificity can be felt, and how emotional and energy-body resonances help guide the work. The talk closes by shifting from what an image can give to what it wants, with examples of reverence, compassion, gratitude, and a careful attention to relationship rather than interpretation.
short_summary: A detailed map of how to work with images in imaginal practice, with emphasis on resonance, posture, and relationship.

chapters.title: Mindfulness of Images
chapters.description: The talk begins by defining imaginal practice as mindfulness applied to images, not fantasy, and by setting up the basic orientation of attending, tuning, and opening.

chapters.title: How an Image Is Sensed
chapters.description: Burbea outlines the first three ways of working with an image: the sense modality through which it is known, whether it wants to fill out, and the character or flavor that can be felt in it.

chapters.title: Specificity in Experience
chapters.description: Using birdsong as sacred text, he shows that the important thing may be the precise depth, beauty, and world-quality of an image rather than any literal meaning or tradition.

chapters.title: Detail and Resonance
chapters.description: He contrasts focusing only on visual detail with focusing only on emotional and energetic resonance, and explains why imaginal practice holds both together.

chapters.title: Signals in the Energy Body
chapters.description: Burbea lists the kinds of energetic cues that suggest an image is 'right' for practice, including alignment, opening, softening, energizing, well-being, harmonization, and contoured body responses.

chapters.title: Posture and the Wounded Bird
chapters.description: A story about a wounded bird image shows how bodily posture can matter, and how a feared image may reveal unexpected love when met in the right way.

chapters.title: Views and Conceptual Frames
chapters.description: He turns to thoughts and views, warning against reducing images to psychology, labels, or advice, and emphasizing play, responsiveness, and the power of the conceptual frame.

chapters.title: What the Image Wants
chapters.description: The final movement asks what the image wants, with examples of trust, benevolence, compassion, and the possibility that an image invites a different perspective or a different kind of love.

chapters.title: Three Ways to Relate
chapters.description: He closes by sketching three stances toward an imaginal figure: behold it, enter into it, or feel oneself being seen by it.

title: Opening, Tuning, and Relating to the Imaginal: Instructions (1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) The set of talks and meditations from this course outlines the foundations and some of the possibilities for opening up a practice of the imaginal. Please note that this set forms a progressively unfolding series of teachings, so the talks and practices will probably be more fully understood and absorbed if they are taken in order.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea outlines how imaginal practice in this retreat will be approached through meditation rather than concretizing images, with a strong emphasis on tuning to the energy body. He then lays out several ways images can arise: from samadhi, emptiness, emotional upset, deliberate invocation, dream and daydream material, and even the world seen as image. The talk keeps returning to a quiet practical question: how to meet images without literalizing them, and how to let them deepen practice.
short_summary: A practical introduction to imaginal practice, centered on energy body awareness, emptiness, and the many ways images can emerge in meditation and life.

chapters.title: Practice context
chapters.description: The talk begins by framing the retreat’s approach to imaginal practice and why it differs from more concretizing methods.

chapters.title: Energy body emphasis
chapters.description: He explains why the retreat leans toward subtle meditative work with the energy body rather than spending most of the time with images themselves.

chapters.title: Seven entry points
chapters.description: He introduces a practical list of ways images can be elicited or received, beginning with samadhi and energy-body attention.

chapters.title: Liquidity and emptiness
chapters.description: Using an alchemical maxim about everything becoming liquid, he links insubstantial perception, emptiness practice, and imaginal fertility.

chapters.title: Emotion as doorway
chapters.description: He shows how emotional upset, low-energy contraction, and the inner critic can all constellate images that carry an intelligence of their own.

chapters.title: Deliberate imagining
chapters.description: He continues through deliberate invocation of figures such as Jesus, Kuan Yin, John Coltrane, Nelson Mandela, and Etty Hillesum, including the role of intention and metta.

chapters.title: Places and daydreams
chapters.description: He turns to imaginative spaces, narrative journeys, and the way ordinary daydreaming can sometimes become meaningful practice.

chapters.title: The world as image
chapters.description: The talk closes by widening imaginal practice to perception itself, including seeing the world as image and noticing where mythos is already alive in life.

title: What is an 'Image'? (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) The set of talks and meditations from this course outlines the foundations and some of the possibilities for opening up a practice of the imaginal. Please note that this set forms a progressively unfolding series of teachings, so the talks and practices will probably be more fully understood and absorbed if they are taken in order.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea continues a careful unpacking of what he means by “image,” moving from simple definitions into a more subtle view of how images shape perception, identity, and practice. He contrasts imaginal seeing with analytic reduction and with bare attention, and he uses examples from Jewish mysticism, Lurianic Kabbalah, Greek myth, and tantric practice to show how images can be both fabricated and deeply real. The talk gradually opens into a broader claim: that the way we look participates in what appears, and that this can support soulfulness rather than flatten experience.
short_summary: A detailed exploration of image, myth, and perception as mutually shaping and potentially soul-making.

chapters.title: What image means
chapters.description: He begins by clarifying that “image” here is not limited to visuals, and may involve resonance, dialogue, ambiguity, and poetic depth.

chapters.title: Poetic and archetypal
chapters.description: He distinguishes one-off images from recurring constellations, and introduces the tendency toward archetypal, irreducible, soul-bearing images.

chapters.title: Against flattening
chapters.description: Using dependent origination and modern reductionism as examples, he argues that analytic breakdown can be useful but can also flatten what is alive and complex.

chapters.title: Fabrication and divinity
chapters.description: He uses emptiness, the Zohar, and a story about writing a book to show a middle way between taking imaginal figures as objectively real and dismissing them as mere products of the mind.

chapters.title: Shards and views
chapters.description: Through Lurianic Kabbalah he develops the idea that perception and interpretation help create new faces of the divine, and that image arises together with the way of looking.

chapters.title: Telos and destiny
chapters.description: He explores how images can carry a future-facing pull, citing James Hillman and discussing how practice may involve more than dissolving suffering.

chapters.title: Myth, memory, papanca
chapters.description: He broadens the scope of image to include myths and memories, and contrasts this with papanca, where experience becomes literalized, contracted, and shorn of resonance.

chapters.title: Stages and cross-pollination
chapters.description: He describes how images unfold gradually, seed one another, and can appear through encounters with people, stories, and cultural material that become imaginally alive.

chapters.title: Seeing as image
chapters.description: He links imaginal perception with tantra, contrasts it with bare attention, and ends by suggesting that lightly held conceptual frameworks can deepen soulfulness and soul-making.

title: What is an 'Image'? (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) The set of talks and meditations from this course outlines the foundations and some of the possibilities for opening up a practice of the imaginal. Please note that this set forms a progressively unfolding series of teachings, so the talks and practices will probably be more fully understood and absorbed if they are taken in order.

title: A Little Exercise for the Imagination
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) The set of talks and meditations from this course outlines the foundations and some of the possibilities for opening up a practice of the imaginal. Please note that this set forms a progressively unfolding series of teachings, so the talks and practices will probably be more fully understood and absorbed if they are taken in order.
podcast_description: In this short guided talk, Rob Burbea leads a series of simple imaginal exercises using a peach, an apple, a rose, a bedroom, a childhood home, a loved one, the seaside, a forest, and a piece of music. The point is not to produce vivid visual pictures, but to notice the felt sense, emotional tone, and energetic resonance that can come with an image. He then reflects on why this matters, especially for people who think they “don’t have images,” and suggests that imaginal sensitivity can be trained.
short_summary: A guided exploration of imaginal practice, emphasizing felt sense and emotional resonance over visual detail.

chapters.title: Settling In
chapters.description: The talk begins with a brief settling of posture and attention into the body and energy body.

chapters.title: The Peach Exercise
chapters.description: A peach is used to explore how imagination can involve texture, smell, taste, and subtle felt presence.

chapters.title: Apple and Rose
chapters.description: The practice shifts to an apple and then a rose, noticing how different senses and resonances may come forward.

chapters.title: Rooms and Home
chapters.description: The bedroom and the room you are in are brought into imagination, along with their mood, atmosphere, and relational feel.

chapters.title: Childhood House
chapters.description: An earlier home is used to deepen the practice, including entering rooms and noticing emotional associations.

chapters.title: Someone Loved
chapters.description: The imagery turns to a loved person, emphasizing their presence, personhood, and the particular quality of love felt toward them.

chapters.title: Seaside and Forest
chapters.description: Two larger scenes, a solitary beach and an autumn forest, are used to include movement, bodily sensation, and the atmosphere of the scene.

chapters.title: Music in Imagination
chapters.description: A familiar piece of music is imagined, with attention to how it shifts energy, mood, and emotional texture.

chapters.title: What the Exercise Shows
chapters.description: Burbea explains that the exercise is meant to show that imagination is broader than visual imagery and that imaginal sensitivity can be cultivated.

title: Energy Body: Instructions (2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) The set of talks and meditations from this course outlines the foundations and some of the possibilities for opening up a practice of the imaginal. Please note that this set forms a progressively unfolding series of teachings, so the talks and practices will probably be more fully understood and absorbed if they are taken in order.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea lays out two core practices for the retreat: developing the energy body as a basis for samadhi, and learning to meet emotions directly in the energy body, especially along the midline. He then shows how these practices relate to a wider Dharma arc: cultivating wholesome heart qualities, understanding how emotions are fabricated, and using attention skillfully with difficult states. Near the end, he begins to connect this emotional work with imaginal practice and explains how images can be held, tuned, and noticed for their effects on the body.
short_summary: A clear introduction to energy-body practice as the foundation for samadhi, emotional awareness, and later imaginal work.

chapters.title: Energy body as foundation
chapters.description: Burbea explains why the energy body will be central throughout the course and how it supports samadhi, metta, emptiness, and imaginal practice.

chapters.title: What emotions include
chapters.description: He defines emotion broadly as both mind and energy body, then places emotional practice in the larger aim of cultivating the heart and letting go of unhelpful habits.

chapters.title: Sensitivity and handling
chapters.description: The talk turns to growing sensitivity to subtle and intense emotions, and to learning how to hold difficult feeling without being overwhelmed.

chapters.title: Midline attention practice
chapters.description: Burbea introduces the second practice: placing steady, lightly held attention on emotional energy along the midline while keeping the whole body in the background.

chapters.title: Allowing and easing
chapters.description: He describes allowing as a practical shift in relationship to sensation, including how deep allowing can soften or dissolve difficulty, and how to check self-judgment.

chapters.title: Opening to images
chapters.description: The talk links emotional practice to imaginal practice, including the role of spontaneous images and deliberate invocation of loving figures.

chapters.title: Three aspects of attention
chapters.description: Burbea distinguishes directing, energizing, and tuning attention, showing how these capacities work in samadhi, emotion work, and imaginal practice.

chapters.title: Two practices, fluidly
chapters.description: He closes by restating the two principal practices, how to shift between them, and how images can be noticed for their effects on the energy body.

title: Energy, Image, Emotion (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) The set of talks and meditations from this course outlines the foundations and some of the possibilities for opening up a practice of the imaginal. Please note that this set forms a progressively unfolding series of teachings, so the talks and practices will probably be more fully understood and absorbed if they are taken in order.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea explores how difficult emotion, psychic energy, and image interact in imaginal practice. Using anger, loneliness, and artistic examples, he shows how an image can release trapped energy, give suffering a place, or transform a feeling without simply erasing it. The talk then widens into a discussion of views: how different ways of looking shape experience, and how Dharma practice can cultivate flexibility rather than a single fixed approach.
short_summary: A talk on how emotion, energy, and image co-arise, and how changing one’s view can open a wider range of experience.

chapters.title: Emotion and trapped energy
chapters.description: Rob begins by describing difficult emotion as energy caught in an unhelpful vortex and introduces the basic connection between emotion, image, and the energy body.

chapters.title: Anger and release
chapters.description: Using anger as the main example, he distinguishes anger from ill will and shows how recognizing anger can free energy and even expand awareness.

chapters.title: Default moods and hidden energy
chapters.description: He turns to habitual low energy or agitation, suggesting these states can hide unrecognized psychic energy that needs to be felt and allowed to move.

chapters.title: Coltrane and image
chapters.description: A story about John Coltrane and Ravi Shankar illustrates how intense expression can be mistaken for anger and how images can carry a larger emotional range than simple labels allow.

chapters.title: Meaning beyond emotion
chapters.description: Rob explains that images can hold shade, depth, duty, and soulfulness, and that they may transform loneliness or pain into something more spacious and meaningful.

chapters.title: Transformation and assumptions
chapters.description: He notes that emotions can dissolve, sweeten, or be held by an image, and invites listeners to notice the assumptions they bring to these shifts.

chapters.title: Causes and transpersonal feeling
chapters.description: The talk questions the habit of demanding a psychological cause for every feeling, bringing in William Blake and the idea that some emotions may be influxes from beyond ordinary personal explanation.

chapters.title: Mutual conditioning
chapters.description: Rob reframes emotion, energy, and image as dependently co-arising, emphasizing that views about what is primary affect what unfolds in practice.

chapters.title: Flexible Dharma views
chapters.description: He closes by contrasting a monistic conception of practice with a broader Dharma of many ways of looking, where non-clinging is understood as a range of modes of awareness that reveal emptiness and widen freedom.

title: Energy, Image, Emotion (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) The set of talks and meditations from this course outlines the foundations and some of the possibilities for opening up a practice of the imaginal. Please note that this set forms a progressively unfolding series of teachings, so the talks and practices will probably be more fully understood and absorbed if they are taken in order.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea explores how emotions, energy, image, and view interrelate in imaginal practice, with an emphasis on the felt life of the energy body. He distinguishes between cultivating calm and pleasure, and simply attending to emotions as they arise, then lays out a larger framework for working with emotions: feeling them, tolerating them, cultivating wholesome qualities, letting go of what is unfruitful, and understanding how they are conditioned. The talk moves into practical guidance on attention, allowing, and the use of helpful images, including several vivid retreat examples where imaginal figures transform pain and shift the energy body.
short_summary: A teaching on working with emotion through the energy body, with practical guidance on attention, allowing, and imaginal images.

chapters.title: Opening the Nexus
chapters.description: Burbea introduces the talk as an exploration of the nexus between emotion, energy, image, and view, and briefly recaps the energy body emphasis of the retreat.

chapters.title: Why Train the Energy Body
chapters.description: He explains the energy body as an experience rather than a belief, and describes how cultivating it supports samadhi, emotional sensitivity, and imaginal practice.

chapters.title: Two Practice Directions
chapters.description: He distinguishes between tending toward well-being in samadhi and simply attending to emotions as they show themselves in the energy body.

chapters.title: Five Aims With Emotion
chapters.description: Burbea lays out five broad aims in emotional practice: sensitivity, capacity to bear, cultivation of wholesome qualities, release of what is unfruitful, inclusion in imaginal practice, and understanding conditioning.

chapters.title: Working With Attention
chapters.description: He turns to the practicalities of sustaining delicate attention, widening awareness, balancing mindfulness against emotion, and recognizing that the relationship to the emotion matters as much as the emotion itself.

chapters.title: Helpful Images in Practice
chapters.description: Burbea explains how deliberate or spontaneous images can support difficult emotion, and reads a retreatant's account of pain transformed into ecstatic pleasure through an image of being kissed.

chapters.title: Lion, Divinity, and Energy
chapters.description: He offers further examples, including a lion healing liver pain and a figure of Ramana Maharshi arising from a sense of divinity, showing how images can open and sometimes need smoothing.

chapters.title: Options and Otherness
chapters.description: He closes by summarizing options for difficult energy, then reflects on how imaginal figures can be deliberately engaged and how their felt Otherness gives images vitality and depth.

title: Energy Body: Instructions (1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) The set of talks and meditations from this course outlines the foundations and some of the possibilities for opening up a practice of the imaginal. Please note that this set forms a progressively unfolding series of teachings, so the talks and practices will probably be more fully understood and absorbed if they are taken in order.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea introduces the retreat’s central practice as working with the energy body: a whole-body field of felt experience that can support samadhi, emotional skillfulness, and imaginal practice. He distinguishes sensation-based body awareness from more conceptual body contemplation, then spends most of the talk surveying practical ways to sense, shape, and play with the energy body through attention, breath, and imagery. The talk ends with concrete advice for working with pain, discomfort, and pleasantness, and with a reminder that the practice is meant to be light, experimental, and gentle.
short_summary: A practical introduction to energy-body practice, samadhi, and playful ways of working with breath, imagery, discomfort, and ease.

chapters.title: Setting the course
chapters.description: Burbea introduces the retreat instructions and explains why there is so much material to absorb slowly and in order.

chapters.title: Three body modes
chapters.description: He distinguishes sensation awareness, conceptual contemplation of the body, and the more direct work of energy-body sensitivity.

chapters.title: What energy body means
chapters.description: He defines the energy body as a phenomenological way of sensing bodily experience, not as a metaphysical belief.

chapters.title: Samadhi and well-being
chapters.description: He describes samadhi as whole-body awareness that becomes bright, restful, harmonious, and useful for practice.

chapters.title: Playful practice
chapters.description: He encourages experimenting without a right-or-wrong attitude and then begins the guided meditation with posture and body scanning.

chapters.title: Opening the field
chapters.description: The meditation expands awareness into a space slightly larger than the body and asks listeners to sense its tone, texture, and vibration.

chapters.title: Breath and centers
chapters.description: He explores breath-energy centered in the heart, solar plexus, hara, and head, emphasizing comfort and direct feeling over doctrine.

chapters.title: Lines and light
chapters.description: The practice shifts to vertical and radiating lines of energy, then to a cloud of light and a body of light as further imaginal options.

chapters.title: Survey of possibilities
chapters.description: He reviews the meditation as a set of options, including metta and other figures, while keeping direct felt sensing primary.

title: Imaginal Practice: Doorways and Directions (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) The set of talks and meditations from this course outlines the foundations and some of the possibilities for opening up a practice of the imaginal. Please note that this set forms a progressively unfolding series of teachings, so the talks and practices will probably be more fully understood and absorbed if they are taken in order.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea reflects on imaginal practice as more than working with images: he argues for a broader framework in which imagination can function as a way of knowing, and in which self, Dharma, and cosmos are all open to reinterpretation. The talk moves from cultural history and philosophy into practical guidance for the retreat, including why the energy body will be central and why the aim is to learn ways of working rather than collect experiences. He repeatedly returns to questions of emptiness, fantasy, soulfulness, and the range of what can count as sacred.
short_summary: A wide-ranging introduction to imaginal practice, reframing imagination, self, Dharma, and cosmos through emptiness, soulfulness, and ways of looking.

chapters.title: Recalling the framework
chapters.description: He briefly reviews the retreat’s earlier themes and says the most important issue is the conceptual framework brought to imaginal practice.

chapters.title: Why imagination matters
chapters.description: He sketches the cultural and philosophical background, arguing that imagination has been too narrowly understood and that it can open a larger sense of sacredness and reality.

chapters.title: Ways of knowing
chapters.description: He names imagination as a possible mode of knowing, introduces the mundus imaginalis, and points to a wider philosophy that can hold the imaginal seriously.

chapters.title: Key themes ahead
chapters.description: He lays out the retreat’s core terms—soulfulness, soulmaking, cosmopoesis, and the relation between imagination and practice—while distinguishing this work from therapy and related modalities.

chapters.title: Self and Dharma
chapters.description: He turns to emptiness, the malleability of self, the pervasion of fantasy in our lives, and how these insights may change what we mean by the Dharma.

chapters.title: Ways of looking
chapters.description: He reframes conventional Dharma injunctions as temporary modes of perception and uses this to explore flexibility, non-clinging, and the emptiness of appearances.

chapters.title: Learning the tools
chapters.description: He urges listeners to value understanding and durable practice skills over unusual experiences, and notes that familiar instructions like breath focus are often harder than they sound.

chapters.title: Energy body and pace
chapters.description: He explains that the first days will focus on the energy body, recommends keeping notes, and asks listeners to slow down gently to support subtler attention.

title: Imaginal Practice: Doorways and Directions (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) The set of talks and meditations from this course outlines the foundations and some of the possibilities for opening up a practice of the imaginal. Please note that this set forms a progressively unfolding series of teachings, so the talks and practices will probably be more fully understood and absorbed if they are taken in order.
podcast_description: Rob Burbea introduces imaginal practice by mapping the many ways imagination already functions in Dharma, psychology, and culture: as planning, rehearsal, devotion, self-cultivation, and symbolic inquiry. He then turns to the historical suspicion of images in Western thought, contrasting that with a more open view in which images can help loosen fixed ideas of self, world, and practice. Toward the end, he names a deeper aim behind the retreat: not just using images skillfully, but allowing them to participate in a re-enchanting of experience.
short_summary: An introduction to imaginal practice, its uses, its cultural context, and the wider shift in self and world-view it can support.

chapters.title: Opening the terrain
chapters.description: Rob sets out the purpose of the course and names imaginal practice as the topic, framing the talk as an introduction to its directions and doorways.

chapters.title: Traditional uses of image
chapters.description: He surveys practical uses of imagination, from planning and rehearsal to death contemplation in the Satipatthana Sutta and recollection of the Buddha.

chapters.title: Devotion and qualities
chapters.description: He gives examples of sacred imagery, tantric visualization, and mountain practice as ways imagination can evoke compassion, faith, courage, and groundedness.

chapters.title: Helpful and unhelpful fantasy
chapters.description: Rob distinguishes intentional imaginal work from ordinary mind-wandering and papancha, and points to upcoming work with samadhi and the energy body.

chapters.title: Images and frameworks
chapters.description: He argues that the image itself is less important than the relationship to it and the conceptual frame that gives it direction, emphasizing openness and trust.

chapters.title: Cultural suspicion of images
chapters.description: Rob traces a history of Western denigration of imagination through psychotherapy, doctrine, and philosophy, using figures like Socrates, Winnicott, Nicaea, and Corbin to show how image has been narrowed or dismissed.

chapters.title: Why people resist
chapters.description: He names common objections to imaginal practice, including fears of madness, conflict with accepted reality, tension with suffering-reduction as the only aim, and discomfort with stranger or darker images.

chapters.title: Plural selfhood
chapters.description: Rob describes what draws others in: a sense of multiple selves, the legitimacy of eros and wrath, and a looser self-view that can make emptiness easier to sense.

chapters.title: Re-enchanting the cosmos
chapters.description: He closes by linking imaginal practice to broader shifts in philosophy and tradition, including tantra and the midrashic condition, and names re-enchanting the cosmos as a deep reason for the retreat.

title: Image, Mythos, Dharma (Part Three)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)
podcast_description: In this talk, Rob Burbea explores the role of image, fantasy, and mythos in Dharma practice, suggesting that these are not side effects of practice but may be central to how we are moved, oriented, and made soulful. He questions the assumption that Buddhism is mainly about reducing suffering, and instead opens a wider view in which beauty, enchantment, devotion, and soul-making also matter. Along the way he draws on Henri Corbin, Jung, Coleridge, Keats, and the Four Noble Truths to ask how different metaphysical assumptions shape what practice means.
short_summary: Rob Burbea examines image and mythos as central forces in Dharma, and asks whether practice may be as much about beauty and soul-making as about suffering's end.

chapters.title: Image Before Meaning
chapters.description: He begins by suggesting that meaningfulness, love, and perception are already shaped by image, fantasy, and mythos.

chapters.title: What Drives Us
chapters.description: He contrasts image-led understanding with common modern assumptions that biology, upbringing, or the past are primary causes.

chapters.title: Corbin and the Imaginal
chapters.description: Using Corbin, Jung, and the alchemical image of liquidity, he reframes imaginal figures as larger than the personal self.

chapters.title: Buddha, Devotion, Fantasy
chapters.description: He reflects on changing frameworks, devotion to the Buddha, and how our sense of awakening is already shaped by fantasy.

chapters.title: The Four Truths Reframed
chapters.description: He argues that the Four Noble Truths are a skeleton that gets filled out by metaphysical assumptions about self, suffering, and liberation.

chapters.title: Beauty and Enchantment
chapters.description: He shifts from a purely therapeutic view of practice toward beauty, enchantment, and the possibility that Dharma itself is a mythos to live by.

chapters.title: Image as Epiphany
chapters.description: With Coleridge and Keats, he describes image as revealing the eternal in the temporal and names soul-making as a possible aim of practice.

chapters.title: Broadening Practice
chapters.description: He closes by asking whether awakening is more open-ended than usually assumed, and how imaginal figures may shape life without needing literal expression.

title: Image, Mythos, Dharma (Part Two)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)
podcast_description: Rob Burbea explores why imaginal practice can matter in Dharma, while also acknowledging the fears it raises around literalism, sanity, and drifting away from reality. He moves from early Buddhist examples like death contemplation and recollecting the Buddha to a wider critique of modern assumptions about what counts as real. The talk then opens into a threefold view of practice: simple mindfulness, deeper practices that fabricate less, and a third path that respects image, fantasy, and mythos as part of spiritual life.
short_summary: A careful argument for imaginal practice as part of Dharma, set against modern ideas of reality and mindfulness.

chapters.title: Setting Up the Question
chapters.description: Burbea introduces the theme, names the discomfort some people feel, and begins by describing imaginal practice as something that often arises in a sensitive one-to-one setting.

chapters.title: Healthy and Unhealthy Imagining
chapters.description: He addresses fears about madness by contrasting nuanced imaginal relationship with literal hallucination, drawing on Mary Watkins and Percival's Narrative.

chapters.title: Metaphor, Canon, and Presence
chapters.description: He uses examples of holy war, death contemplation, and recollecting the Buddha to argue that imagination is already present in Buddhist practice and that the issue is often literalism.

chapters.title: Reality and Assumptions
chapters.description: Burbea distinguishes objective and experiential reality, then breaks down the hidden metaphysical assumptions carried by ontology, epistemology, and cosmology.

chapters.title: Modernism and Matter
chapters.description: He critiques the modern assumption that matter is the only reality and uses physics to suggest that even science does not support such a simple picture.

chapters.title: Reading the Pali Canon
chapters.description: The talk turns to the Buddha's path as a transcendent one that fabricates less, with mindfulness as support rather than the final aim and the deathless as the end point.

chapters.title: Three Paths of Practice
chapters.description: He lays out three possibilities: bare mindfulness, stronger practices that reduce fabrication further, and practices that openly honor image, fantasy, and mythos.

chapters.title: Mythos, Love, and Meaning
chapters.description: Burbea concludes by showing how emptiness can open imaginal practice, how mythos is already part of religious life, and how love and meaningfulness often depend on imaginative amplitude.

title: Guided Meditation 1
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)
podcast_description: This guided meditation begins with settling posture and releasing obvious tension in the face, throat, shoulders, chest, and belly. It then widens into a larger field of awareness, first as a spacious bodily sense and then as a bright, energetic space that can be supported by imagined lines of energy and, optionally, the breath. The practice gradually becomes more imaginal, suggesting a radiant body of light and inviting the listener to notice what feels helpful rather than forcing any one visualization.
short_summary: A sparse guided meditation that moves from posture and relaxation into spacious, luminous, imaginal awareness.

chapters.title: Settling the posture
chapters.description: The meditation begins by establishing an upright but relaxed posture and sensing a balance of wakefulness and softness.

chapters.title: Releasing tension
chapters.description: Attention moves through the face, throat, neck, shoulders, back, chest, and belly, encouraging gentle relaxation and release.

chapters.title: Grounding in contact
chapters.description: The practice widens to simple sensations in the hands, backside, legs, and feet, grounding awareness in immediate bodily contact.

chapters.title: Opening the field
chapters.description: Awareness is invited to expand beyond the body into a larger space filled with bright, sensitive presence.

chapters.title: Restoring spacious presence
chapters.description: The talk emphasizes repeatedly re-expanding attention when it shrinks, using a wind-and-sail image to support an open, filled space.

chapters.title: Imagining energy lines
chapters.description: A central line of energy is introduced, then extended into additional lines down the legs, with an emphasis on sensing what feels supportive.

chapters.title: Breath and support
chapters.description: The meditation offers the breath as an optional support for the whole-body field, including gentle expansion on the in-breath and relaxation on the out-breath.

chapters.title: Experimenting with form
chapters.description: Listeners are encouraged to try different combinations of breath, energy lines, and imagination, always returning to the felt sense of the whole space.

chapters.title: Body of light
chapters.description: The practice deepens into a radiant or translucent body of light, with color and luminosity left open to the listener's own response.

title: Image, Mythos, Dharma (Part One)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)
podcast_description: Rob Burbea opens by framing image, fantasy, and mythos as useful, non-pejorative parts of practice, then turns to a series of examples showing how imaginal material can carry love, depth, and transformation. He moves from vivid inner figures like a thicket and blue bird, to a frightening black devil man, to subtler images shaped by work, lineage, place, and healing. The talk then widens into a careful discussion of how images function in Dharma practice, how they differ from fixed symbols, and why they can be individually real without being publicly verifiable.
short_summary: An exploration of imaginal practice: how images, fantasy, and mythos can reveal depth, healing, and soulfulness in Dharma.

chapters.title: Opening the territory
chapters.description: He introduces image, fantasy, and mythos as a broad field of practice and explains that he will use examples rather than strict definitions.

chapters.title: Blue bird and thicket
chapters.description: A personal meditation image of a thicket and blue bird shows how imaginal material can be subtle, moving, and not reducible to interpretation.

chapters.title: Devil, train, and change
chapters.description: A frightening recurring figure becomes an ally over years, followed by an ordinary train image that reveals fatigue, restlessness, and underlying peace.

chapters.title: Embodied tradition
chapters.description: He uses music and the feel of the instrument to show how image can be tactile, kinetic, and infused by lineage.

chapters.title: Phoenix and healing
chapters.description: A phoenix image is linked to shame around an old relationship and shows how an imaginal figure can reframe memory with love.

chapters.title: Fantasy in life and place
chapters.description: He broadens the discussion to shared fantasy in relationships, then to Gaia House and a Thai monastery as places made vivid through love and narrative.

chapters.title: Visionary seeing
chapters.description: William Blake and tantric visualization are used to show imagination as a deliberate way of perceiving the world.

chapters.title: Condensed meaning
chapters.description: Drawing on Ezra Pound and John Keats, he describes image as compressed emotional-intellectual complexity and soul-making through relationship.

chapters.title: Literalism and reality
chapters.description: He warns against literalizing archetypal images, argues that images are not flat symbols, and distinguishes individual truth from public certainty.

title: In Praise of Restlessness
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) This talk is the third part of a talk in three parts. (Part 1 is "Questioning Awakening"; Part 2 is "Buddhism Beyond Modernism".)
podcast_description: Rob Burbea continues a long three-part talk by asking what it means to let the Dharma be shaped by uncertainty, myth, and imagination rather than by fixed claims. He distinguishes ordinary restlessness from the kind of restlessness that belongs to inquiry, creativity, and a life that keeps opening. The talk moves from critique of dogma and nostalgia for origins into a wider case for Dharma as art: open-ended, beautiful, and sustained by desire, longing, and changing ways of seeing.
short_summary: A meditation on restlessness as a force for inquiry, and on Dharma as an open-ended, artistic way of knowing.

chapters.title: Setting the frame
chapters.description: Rob briefly recaps the first two talks and introduces the central claim that Dharma always carries hidden assumptions about what is real and how it is known.

chapters.title: Metaphysics and myth
chapters.description: He unpacks how metaphysics and mythos quietly shape practice, then asks what happens when those hidden supports are brought to the foreground.

chapters.title: Homelessness and inquiry
chapters.description: The talk turns to images of homelessness, seekerhood, and fugitive running, before distinguishing agitation from the more fruitful restlessness of inquiry.

chapters.title: Creative unrest
chapters.description: Using Critchley, Picasso, and alchemy, he argues that some disturbance is part of real work and that the path may need heat rather than premature settling.

chapters.title: Desire on the path
chapters.description: He explores desire as a legitimate force in science, religion, and practice, drawing on Jung, the Buddha, the Dalai Lama, St. John of the Cross, and others.

chapters.title: Pothos and endless work
chapters.description: The talk widens into longing, beauty, and the sense that practice can be driven by an unending task rather than a final endpoint.

chapters.title: Beauty and enchantment
chapters.description: He suggests that people often practice not only to reduce suffering but for wonder, beauty, and a more enchanted relation to the world.

chapters.title: Dharma as art
chapters.description: The closing section develops the strongest form of the argument: Dharma as art, with poetic truth, multiple readings, and a liberating openness that cannot be boxed in.

title: Buddhism Beyond Modernism
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) This talk is the second part of a talk in three parts. (Part 1 is "Questioning Awakening"; Part 3 is "In Praise of Restlessness".)
podcast_description: In this second part of a three-part talk, Rob Burbea examines the assumptions hidden inside Dharma practice: what counts as truth, what counts as real, and what gets left unasked. He moves from the role of ideas and inquiry, through Thomas Kuhn and modern science, into critiques of modernism, bare attention, and reductionist ways of explaining experience. The talk then opens toward imagination, myth, and fantasy as shaping forces in practice, including a careful questioning of the stories we tell about awakening, the Buddha, and liberation itself.
short_summary: Rob Burbea questions the assumptions beneath Dharma, modernism, and the idea of liberation, arguing for a more open, imaginative, and self-aware inquiry.

chapters.title: Setting up the inquiry
chapters.description: Burbea explains that this talk will not follow a linear teaching style, but will instead open up the assumptions beneath Dharma practice and awakening.

chapters.title: Ideas and their power
chapters.description: He defines ideas as the lenses shaping perception, and shows how concepts, fantasies, and mythos can open life or close it down.

chapters.title: Freedom to inquire
chapters.description: Using Einstein and the Gaia House sign, he argues that real inquiry needs freedom, and that inquiry must include what feels taboo or hard to question.

chapters.title: Kuhn and the Dharma
chapters.description: Burbea uses Thomas Kuhn's account of paradigms and revolutions to ask whether Dharma itself can become trapped in conceptual boxes.

chapters.title: Modernism examined
chapters.description: He turns to Richard Tarnas and sketches modernism as a worldview shaped by materialism, objectivity, and a narrow sense of what counts as real.

chapters.title: Breaking the vessels
chapters.description: With Heisenberg, Einstein, and Lurianic Kabbalah, he explores how crises shatter inherited structures and make room for new ways of holding experience.

chapters.title: Styles of inquiry
chapters.description: He contrasts Apollonian, Dionysian, and Hermes-like modes of inquiry, while questioning the modern preference for bare attention and closed systems.

chapters.title: Multiple worlds of meaning
chapters.description: Burbea widens the frame to show how different metaphysics, stories, and images generate different worlds, and why no single discourse can claim final authority.

chapters.title: Liberation and fantasy
chapters.description: He closes by arguing that liberation, awakening, and the Buddha are always entangled with fantasy, interpretation, and cosmology, and that recognizing this is part of honest practice.

title: Questioning Awakening
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) This talk is the first part of a talk in three parts. (Part 2 is "Buddhism Beyond Modernism"; Part 3 is "In Praise of Restlessness".)
podcast_description: Rob Burbea opens a three-part talk by questioning the assumptions that sit underneath Dharma language, practice, and the idea of awakening. He compares several different “packages” of Dharma—mindfulness as coping, existential acceptance, machine-like optimization, transcendence, emptiness, and more—to show how each one carries its own hidden view of reality. The talk keeps returning to the same pressure point: whether we are actually free to inquire into what we take for granted, or whether our views are quietly shaping what practice can be.
short_summary: A wide-ranging inquiry into the hidden assumptions behind Dharma, awakening, and what we think practice is for.

chapters.title: Opening the inquiry
chapters.description: Burbea frames the talk as the first of three and explains that he wants to question assumptions beneath familiar Dharma teaching rather than offer practical tips.

chapters.title: Many versions of awakening
chapters.description: He surveys the range of views on awakening, from its disappearance to its dilution or rigid fixation, and asks what is driving those shifts.

chapters.title: Four truths, many goals
chapters.description: Using the Four Noble Truths, he shows how different traditions and modern readers can mean very different things by the path, dukkha, and liberation.

chapters.title: What do we want?
chapters.description: He turns to the aims of practice—presence, simplicity, reducing suffering, and different styles of striving—and asks whether these are enough or even coherent.

chapters.title: Presence and the now
chapters.description: Burbea questions the popular ideal of savoring the moment and living life to the full, arguing that it carries hidden assumptions about reality and significance.

chapters.title: Mindfulness and reality
chapters.description: He examines mindfulness as a coping strategy and contrasts taking meditation states as final truths with investigating how perceptions arise and cease.

chapters.title: Ontology behind practice
chapters.description: The talk broadens into philosophy, with ontology, epistemology, and cosmology presented as unavoidable background assumptions in any practice path.

chapters.title: Modern materialist models
chapters.description: He critiques the machine model of the universe and mind, drawing on Heidegger, Pascal, and Heisenberg to show how modern assumptions shape Dharma.

chapters.title: Questioning hidden views
chapters.description: Burbea closes by stressing that imagination, therapy narratives, and even claims of being non-conceptual all rest on views that should themselves be questioned.

title: Creative Samadhi
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)
podcast_description: Rob Burbea reopens the meaning of samadhi beyond narrow concentration practice, showing how it belongs within a larger path of insight and cultivation. He argues that practice is an art of deepening perception, nourishing the heart, and learning to work skillfully with attention, effort, and letting go. After a conceptual overview, he offers a brief guided exploration of whole-body awareness and energetic texture, then closes by reflecting on how openness, beauty, and everyday doing belong in the path.
short_summary: A talk on samadhi as whole-body, heart-nourished practice rather than mere concentration.

chapters.title: Opening the question
chapters.description: Burbea introduces samadhi, asks what the path is really for, and invites listeners to question inherited assumptions about practice.

chapters.title: The path goes somewhere
chapters.description: He critiques a thin reading of 'just be present' and reframes the path as aiming at the undoing of fundamental delusion.

chapters.title: Two wings of practice
chapters.description: The talk turns to the paired roles of insight and cultivation, and to wholesome qualities like generosity, kindness, and mindfulness as sources of nourishment.

chapters.title: Samadhi as wholeness
chapters.description: Samadhi is described as a spectrum of deepening consciousness that involves the whole body, unification, and a felt sense of well-being.

chapters.title: Why focus is not enough
chapters.description: He lays out common one-pointed methods, then shows how mindfulness, patience, kindness, and the wider field of practice matter more than mere steadiness.

chapters.title: Ways to deepen
chapters.description: Burbea offers practical refinements: widen the conception of practice, value quality over quantity, allow subtlety, and include the whole body as a background field.

chapters.title: Whole-body experiment
chapters.description: He leads a guided inquiry into relaxing, opening awareness, sensing energy, and using breath or imagined channels to feel the body as a living field.

chapters.title: Space, letting go, and looking
chapters.description: The instructions widen into open awareness, where phenomena arise and pass while attention steadies on space, relationship, and release.

chapters.title: Open heart, open practice
chapters.description: In the closing movement, he questions the ideal of no effort, reflects on doing and desire in life, and returns to the importance of nourishing the heart through gratitude, nature, beauty, and community.

title: Ayya Santacitta, Ayya Santussika, David Loy, Thanissara: Mindfulness and Climate Action : 5
speaker: Thanissara
description: (Unknown) Thanissara with David Loy, Ayya Santussika Bhikkhuni and Ayya Santacitta Bhikkhuni. Unfortunately, Ven. Bhikkhu Bodhi was ill on the day of our call and was not able to join us. We wish him a speedy and thorough recovery. In this call, Ayya Santussika Bhikkhuni mentioned two resources: the international, interfaith effort Our Voices as well as the “Pledge to Mobilize” at the Climate Mobilization. She also invited those interested in taking action together as Buddhists on the national or local/regional level to email her at santussika at gmail.com. Finally, Dave Damm-Luhr mentioned the upcoming film from the same group that brought us “The Overview Effect.” This new film, “Planetary” (see the trailer here) maybe well-timed for action around the March deadline for targets into the UN climate talks that Lou mentioned.
podcast_description: This episode closes a five-part series on mindfulness, Dharma, and climate action with a practical question: how can Buddhist practice become collective response? David Loy frames the climate crisis as a challenge to reexamine Buddhism itself, while Thanissara and Ayya Santussika move the conversation toward organizing, public pressure, and faith-based networks. The talk stays grounded in both inner practice and concrete action, ending with reflections on joy, courage, and continuing the work together.
short_summary: A closing conversation on Buddhist practice, collective action, and climate organizing, moving from Dharma framing to concrete steps and shared resolve.

chapters.title: Opening the final call
chapters.description: Thanissara opens the last session of the series, notes Bhikkhu Bodhi’s absence, and names the talk’s focus on turning reflection into action.

chapters.title: What can we actually do?
chapters.description: Lu introduces the central question and points to hope through existing signs of change, especially renewable energy and political engagement.

chapters.title: Reexamining Buddhist practice
chapters.description: David Loy argues that Buddhism needs to clarify what awakening means in a climate crisis, challenging both transcendence-as-escape and purely psychological inwardness.

chapters.title: The bodhisattva path
chapters.description: Loy develops the bodhisattva path as a double practice of meditation and engagement, linking wisdom, compassion, and the willingness to act without certainty of results.

chapters.title: Building a Buddhist movement
chapters.description: Ayya Santussika describes how Buddhists can organize locally and nationally, drawing on the People's Climate March, faith coalitions, divestment, and voter engagement.

chapters.title: Precepts, urgency, and earth
chapters.description: The discussion turns to ethical responsibility, the first two precepts, climate deadlines, fossil-fuel dependence, and the Buddha touching the earth as a model for fearless speech and action.

chapters.title: Questions from the sangha
chapters.description: Listeners respond with gratitude, questions about resources, and stories of being moved into new forms of leadership, including local teaching and voting.

chapters.title: Joy, continuity, blessing
chapters.description: The closing section returns to joy as a condition for sustained action, outlines plans for monthly follow-up calls, and ends with a Buddhist blessing.

title: Bonnie Duran, Chas DiCapua, Thanissara, Vinny Ferraro: Mindfulness and Climate Action : 4
speaker: Thanissara
description: (Unknown) Thanissara with Bonnie Duran, Chas DiCapua and Vinny Ferraro.
podcast_description: This conversation moves from climate action as a practical response to climate harm into a deeper inquiry about interdependence, colonization, and embodiment. Bonnie Duran frames climate change through Indigenous knowledge and the Buddhist roots poisons, Chas DiCapua turns to patriarchy, technology, and the need to come back into the body, and Vinny Ferraro brings the discussion toward belonging, grief, and action that arises from listening inwardly. The talk closes with breakout reflections and a merit dedication that widens the circle to animals, human communities, and the earth itself.
short_summary: A three-part Dharma conversation on climate action, Indigenous wisdom, patriarchy, embodiment, and returning to interdependence.

chapters.title: Opening and Introductions
chapters.description: Thanissara welcomes the teachers and outlines the conversation on mindfulness, Dharma, and climate action.

chapters.title: Plant-Based Ethics
chapters.description: The opening teaching argues for reducing animal products by linking factory farming to emissions, land use, water use, and compassion.

chapters.title: Colonization and Root Poisons
chapters.description: Bonnie Duran relates climate change to colonization, greed, hatred, and delusion, and introduces Indigenous ecological knowledge as part of the response.

chapters.title: Rights of Nature
chapters.description: The teaching moves through Pachamama, Bolivia and Ecuador, and a quote from John Mohawk to emphasize humility and the intelligence of the larger whole.

chapters.title: Technology and Patriarchy
chapters.description: Chas DiCapua expands the critique into patriarchy, overvaluing intellect and science, and the devaluing of body, feminine energy, and nature.

chapters.title: Returning to the Body
chapters.description: He brings the talk back to embodied practice, the Satipatthana Sutta, and the idea that connection with the body is the starting point for wise action.

chapters.title: Belonging and Grief
chapters.description: Vinny Ferraro speaks about interdependence, the pain of the world, and how despair can become connection rather than paralysis.

chapters.title: Listening and Action
chapters.description: The teaching becomes more explicitly guided as Vinny leads inward listening, quotes John Seed, Buddhadasa, and Rumi, and frames action as participation in a larger life.

chapters.title: Questions and Dedication
chapters.description: The group responds with questions about discouragement, integrity, and livelihood, then closes with a broad merit dedication and final housekeeping.

title: Catherine McGee, Chris Cullen, James Baraz, Thanissara: Mindfulness and Climate Action : 3
speaker: Thanissara
description: (Unknown) Thanissara with James Baraz, Catherine McGee and Chris Cullen.
podcast_description: This conversation brings together Thanissara, Catherine McGee, Chris Cullen, and James Baraz to explore climate action as a Dharma practice. The talk moves from the urgency of the climate crisis into a steadier question: how to stay grounded, emotionally honest, and still willing to act. Across the episode, the teachers return to reconnection, gratitude, grief, and the kind of view that makes sustained action possible.
short_summary: A Dharma conversation on climate action, emotional resilience, and how practice can support wise engagement.

chapters.title: Opening and Welcome
chapters.description: Thanissara welcomes listeners, introduces the series, and frames the conversation as an invitation to engage climate change in community.

chapters.title: Why This Matters
chapters.description: Lou Leonard outlines the climate context and the need for both systemic and individual responses, then hands to Catherine McGee.

chapters.title: Action and View
chapters.description: Catherine begins by asking where the silence of practice meets the passion of activism, and grounds action in intention, view, and an open system of relationship.

chapters.title: Bone-Jangling Reality
chapters.description: She shares a story of bringing climate data to an old Dharma teacher, using his shaken response to show how practice can hold painful truth without shutting down.

chapters.title: Silence and Fire
chapters.description: Catherine reflects on figures like Nelson Mandela and the possibility of combining depth, steadiness, and justice-oriented energy in action.

chapters.title: Reconnecting With the Earth
chapters.description: Chris takes up Joanna Macy and Chris Johnston's Active Hope as a four-part spiral: gratitude, honoring pain, seeing with new eyes, and going forth.

chapters.title: From Practice to Action
chapters.description: He turns to concrete steps, from home energy use and travel to public conversation and divestment, while stressing that action and contemplation support each other.

chapters.title: James on Inspiration
chapters.description: James speaks about holding an inspiring vision, how Bill McKibben's Earth changed his relationship to climate grief, and why climate work can be treated as Dharma practice.

chapters.title: Questions and Blessing
chapters.description: The discussion continues with audience questions about leverage and institutions, then closes with gratitude, a short announcement, and Catherine's blessing for all beings.

title: Ayya Anandabodhi, Rev. angel Kyodo williams, Susie Harrington, Thanissara: Mindfulness and Climate Action : 2
speaker: Thanissara
description: (Unknown) Thanissara with Rev. angel Kyodo williams, Ayya Anandabodhi Bhikkhuni and Susie Harrington. In this conversation, Susie Harrington mentioned two sources: Kerry Nelson’s offering of online workshops at A Place for Peace as well as Vinit Allen’s Sustainable World Sourcebook. Both offer ways to engage individually but also at the community level.Rev angel Kyodo williams mentioned the site What Is Missing? from Maya Lin offering an interactive experience of all that has been and continues to be lost to which you can add your own accounting.
podcast_description: This conversation brings climate change into the center of Dharma practice, treating it as a spiritual, ethical, and social reality rather than a separate concern. Thanissara moderates reflections from Ayya Anandabodhi, Reverend Angel Kyodo Williams, and Susie Harrington, who each return to the themes of interconnection, the three poisons, systemic harm, grief, and the need for skillful action. The talk moves from naming the crisis and its inequities to practical ways of engaging without collapse, denial, or hopelessness.
short_summary: A Dharma conversation on climate action, interconnection, justice, grief, and practical engagement.

chapters.title: Opening the conversation
chapters.description: Thanissara introduces the panel and frames climate change as a Dharma and justice issue, with a brief grounding breath for everyone joining the call.

chapters.title: Climate as practice
chapters.description: Ayya Anandabodhi speaks about climate change as an ethical and spiritual matter, naming fear, overwhelm, and the need for right action.

chapters.title: Interbeing and the poisons
chapters.description: Ayya develops the teaching of interconnection and links ecological crisis to greed, hatred, and delusion, urging practitioners not to hide behind impermanence.

chapters.title: Justice and systemic harm
chapters.description: Reverend Angel Kyodo Williams expands the lens to colonialism, hyper-capitalism, racism, and the need for sangha to become a deeper form of collective responsibility.

chapters.title: Not looking away
chapters.description: Susie Harrington reflects on truth, grief, extraction, and the courage to face what is happening without turning away, including examples from the Alberta tar sands.

chapters.title: From overwhelm to action
chapters.description: The teachers explore overwhelm as a form of collapse, then point toward compassion, nature, local action, and community resources as ways through it.

chapters.title: Questions and next steps
chapters.description: The discussion turns to breakout groups, an upcoming training, and questions about how to speak about climate change in resistant settings.

chapters.title: Facing resistance
chapters.description: Ayya and Reverend Angel answer questions about engaging people non-threateningly, finding the right community, and speaking from a truthful place.

chapters.title: Closing blessings
chapters.description: The call ends with announcements, final encouragement, and a blessing that returns attention to the body, the earth, and shared good intention.

title: The Four Noble Truths of Responding to Climate Change
speaker: Donald Rothberg
description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center) Continuing our exploration of ethical practice during earth care week. We examine, through the frame work of the Four Noble Truths, the question of the nature of climate change and how to respond as individuals, as communities, nationally and internationally.
podcast_description: Donald Rothberg frames climate change through the Four Noble Truths, moving from an honest reckoning with suffering into the causes, possibilities, and pathways for response. He draws on Buddhist ethics, Thich Nhat Hanh, Joanna Macy, Bhikkhu Bodhi, Bill McKibben, Lester Brown, and the Dalai Lama to connect inner practice with collective action. The talk balances sober climate facts with practical encouragement, especially around staying connected, avoiding despair, and taking local and political steps.
short_summary: A Dharma talk that uses the Four Noble Truths to explore the reality of climate change, its causes, and forms of compassionate response.

chapters.title: Ethics and Integrity
chapters.description: Rothberg opens by situating climate work inside ethical practice, integrity, and the three trainings of Buddhism.

chapters.title: Non-harming and Earth Care
chapters.description: He widens the first precept through Thich Nhat Hanh and an Earth Care Week statement to frame ecological harm as a moral concern.

chapters.title: Four Noble Truths Framework
chapters.description: He lays out the Four Noble Truths as the structure for the talk and thanks several teachers who shaped the presentation.

chapters.title: The Bad News
chapters.description: He names climate change as a grave collective suffering, reviews basic climate science, and describes the scale of likely impacts and injustice.

chapters.title: Meeting Distress
chapters.description: After pausing with the weight of the topic, he turns to practices for facing fear, grief, numbness, and shock together.

chapters.title: Roots of the Crisis
chapters.description: He outlines outer and inner causes, including fossil fuels, consumer culture, privilege, fear, distraction, and denial.

chapters.title: Possibility of Transformation
chapters.description: He shifts to the third noble truth, arguing that a sustainable and more just world is possible and pointing to rapid changes already underway.

chapters.title: The Path of Response
chapters.description: He describes personal, community, and national responses, including meditation, ethical living, local organizing, divestment, and broad mobilization.

chapters.title: Questions and Closing
chapters.description: In discussion he returns to compassion, polarization, and staying connected, then closes with dedications and a reminder of shared interdependence.

title: Releasing the Self, Freeing its Demons (Part 3)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)
podcast_description: Rob Burbea explores how images, fantasies, and inner figures shape practice far more than we often notice. He moves from common ways of treating imagery—ignoring it, psychologizing it, or folding it into a tidy self-story—toward a more imaginal approach in which images are met as meaningful, sometimes demanding presences. The talk keeps returning to a quiet test: what happens in the body, the heart, and the sense of self when an image is taken seriously without being literalized?
short_summary: A close look at imaginal practice, and how the way we conceive images can open a wider, more soulful freedom.

chapters.title: Setting the frame
chapters.description: Rob introduces the theme of image and fantasy, and explains that the talk will extend the earlier teaching.

chapters.title: Common responses
chapters.description: He surveys the usual Dharma attitudes toward images: ignore them, notice and release them, or use them to soften judgment.

chapters.title: Psychological readings
