chapters.description: He turns to psychotherapeutic and integrative ways of understanding images as past material, compensation, or parts of a self to be balanced.

chapters.title: Conceiving and experimenting
chapters.description: He shifts from true-versus-false to what different ways of looking lead to, emphasizing mindfulness, bodily resonance, and meaningfulness.

chapters.title: Image as living presence
chapters.description: He asks what changes when images are not judged by their content and when their relation to the body reveals whether they are fruitful.

chapters.title: From emptiness to freedom
chapters.description: The Buddha's teaching on emptiness, together with Eliot and Rilke, frames an experiment in letting images carry more power than the ego usually allows.

chapters.title: Daemons and angels
chapters.description: He develops the idea that images may not come from personal history at all, drawing on Corbin and Hillman to speak of service to psychic persons or daemons.

chapters.title: Risk, myth, and ethics
chapters.description: He addresses the danger of this approach, distinguishes mythic truth from literal belief, and asks how to tell ego-proliferation from something deeper.

chapters.title: What is not just ego
chapters.description: Through examples from Hendrix, Senna, Buddhist polemics, the Buddha's own words, and the myth of Horus, he questions how quickly we dismiss grandeur, desire, and force as mere ego.

title: Jack Kornfield, Ruth King, Tara Brach, Thanissara: Mindfulness and Climate Action : 1
speaker: Thanissara
description: (Unknown) Thanissara with Tara Brach, Ruth King and Jack Kornfield.
podcast_description: This conversation begins with a shared pause and a framing of climate action as Dharma practice, then moves through Tara Brach’s emphasis on interdependence, grief, and love as the basis for response. Ruth King follows by naming climate change as a systemic issue tied to racism, exploitation, and collective responsibility, while Jack Kornfield widens the lens to dukkha, bodhisattva vows, and long-term, non-attached action. The talk closes with questions from participants and practical encouragement to stay connected, organize, and keep practicing together.
short_summary: A Dharma conversation on climate crisis, interdependence, systemic injustice, and compassionate collective action.

chapters.title: Opening and grounding
chapters.description: Thanissara welcomes listeners, frames the conversation, and invites a brief settling into body and breath.

chapters.title: Climate as shared condition
chapters.description: Lou Leonard outlines the climate picture, the scale of the challenge, and the importance of sangha and shared attention.

chapters.title: Presence and belonging
chapters.description: Tara Brach speaks about separation, fear, the unreal other, and the need to meet the earth with love and attention.

chapters.title: Systems and justice
chapters.description: Ruth King links climate harm to greed, racism, and structural oppression, and names mutuality as the basis for action.

chapters.title: Dukkha and bodhisattva path
chapters.description: Jack Kornfield grounds the talk in the Buddha’s first noble truth and then shifts into bodhisattva vows and expansive responsibility.

chapters.title: Act from love
chapters.description: Jack draws on Merton, the Bhagavad Gita, Gary Snyder, and Maha Gosananda to stress non-attachment, love, and step-by-step compassion.

chapters.title: Questions on practice
chapters.description: The teachers respond to questions about anger, activism, and how inner practice and outer action can support each other.

chapters.title: Organizing together
chapters.description: Participants ask about systemic politics, good news, and practical networks, and the teachers name concrete movements and shared responsibility.

chapters.title: Closing blessings
chapters.description: The talk ends with a short blessing for Earth, a reminder to keep gathering, and an invitation to continue the series and the work.

title: Releasing the Self, Freeing its Demons (Part 2)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)
podcast_description: Rob Burbea continues an exploration of imaginal practice, using retreat stories to show how inner figures can be met non-literally and with mindfulness. He moves from the inner critic to a wider inner theater of persons, then widens further to public figures, mythic energies, and the images that shape a life. The talk closes by relating image-work to emotional energy and to the steadiness of samadhi, suggesting that both upset and settledness can open the way to imagery.
short_summary: A talk on meeting inner figures, loosening identification, and working with images as a valid contemplative path.

chapters.title: Picking Up the Thread
chapters.description: He situates the talk as a continuation and says he will return to the material in stages through the day.

chapters.title: Inner Critic, Inner Persons
chapters.description: Through a retreat example, he shows how the inner critic can open into a dialogue with multiple figures rather than a single fixed voice.

chapters.title: Mindfulness and Image
chapters.description: He distinguishes mindfulness with images from literalizing them, and explains how this both protects and vivifies the practice.

chapters.title: Loosening a Solid Self
chapters.description: He places image-work alongside emptiness and mystical dissolution as another way of softening belief in a singular self.

chapters.title: Images as Resources
chapters.description: A series of retreat stories shows images as sources of care, nourishment, power, and soul-level opening, including the grandpa, goddess, dragon, soldier, and mountain lion.

chapters.title: Figures in Culture
chapters.description: He turns to public figures and traditions, including the Thai forest Ajahns, Mandela, Einstein, Bohr, Hendrix, and Coltrane, to show how people can live in the psyche as images.

chapters.title: Wildness and Shame
chapters.description: He considers images that do not fit Buddhist ideals, including Dionysian energy and a story of sexual shame reframed as sacred rather than merely fallen.

chapters.title: Working Deliberately
chapters.description: He offers practical ways to engage images in meditation and notes that upset and samadhi can both make image-work more available.

title: Releasing the Self, Freeing its Demons (Part 1)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)
podcast_description: Rob Burbea questions a familiar Buddhist habit of treating the self as merely a bundle of aggregates, arguing that this can be useful but also narrow, both spiritually and psychologically. He then turns to a more imaginal way of working with experience, using the inner critic as a case study and showing how dialogue, kindness, and a different way of looking can transform what feels oppressive. The talk ends by widening again, with a story about grief and the image of a dead mother, suggesting that what is alive in the psyche may ask for a more spacious and beautiful kind of response.
short_summary: A talk on moving beyond oversimplified ideas of self into a more flexible, imaginal way of relating to inner critic, grief, and psyche.

chapters.title: Questioning the usual view
chapters.description: Burbea begins by naming the common teaching that ego is the problem and self is illusory, then starts to test that framing.

chapters.title: Limits of aggregates
chapters.description: He argues that reducing self to a process of aggregates is not something the Buddha said and can flatten deeper understanding.

chapters.title: A skillful way of looking
chapters.description: The aggregates and emptiness are presented as perspectives that can be used meditatively, not final descriptions of reality.

chapters.title: Why simplicity fails
chapters.description: He turns to modern complexity, eros, art, and personality, suggesting that simplistic teachings can fail to nourish the richness of life.

chapters.title: Modern suffering and self
chapters.description: Burbea notes that much contemporary suffering sits at the level of personality, and asks for other ways of opening the notion of self.

chapters.title: Meeting the inner critic
chapters.description: He introduces the inner critic as a familiar structure and names loving-kindness and insight into emptiness as two ways of softening it.

chapters.title: From deconstructing to relating
chapters.description: The talk shifts to an imaginal move: treating the critic as a figure, entering dialogue, and allowing relationship to change the experience.

chapters.title: The clumsy protector
chapters.description: Burbea suggests the critic may be a protector in disguise, and tells a retreat story in which turning toward it reveals unexpected kindness.

chapters.title: Grief and the mother image
chapters.description: A second story shows how a dead mother can remain alive as an image in the psyche, and how responding to that image can open a freer and more beautiful relation.

title: Meditation: Opening to Aliveness
speaker: Tara Brach
description: (Insight Meditation Community of Washington DC)
podcast_description: This guided meditation begins with settling posture, breath, and intention, then moves into a full-body scan that emphasizes softness, sensitivity, and aliveness. Tara Brach invites an embodied noticing of the senses, sound, and the background of awareness, with repeated reminders that the mind can drift and return without judgment. The practice closes by deepening into the felt earth-body and the spacious silence that holds changing experience.
short_summary: A guided meditation on opening to bodily aliveness, sound, and the awareness that can receive experience without judgment.

chapters.title: Settling the Body
chapters.description: The meditation opens with posture, balance, and a few deliberate breaths to establish alertness and ease.

chapters.title: Intention and Aum
chapters.description: Attention moves to the heart, where she asks about sincere intention and introduces a brief chanting of Aum.

chapters.title: Softening the Face
chapters.description: The practice turns to the face, jaw, mouth, and shoulders, using relaxation to invite receptivity rather than force.

chapters.title: Heart and Belly
chapters.description: Awareness widens through the chest and belly, with the reminder that smiling into experience is a way of making space for it.

chapters.title: Pelvis and Earth Body
chapters.description: The scan continues through the pelvic region, legs, and feet, opening into the sense of the body as a living field and the earth beneath it.

chapters.title: Sound and Presence
chapters.description: Sound is folded into the practice, and the talk points to an alert stillness in the background of hearing and sensing.

chapters.title: Returning Fresh
chapters.description: She names the ordinary drift into story and offers a simple, nonjudgmental return to the senses and the breath.

chapters.title: Deepening Into Silence
chapters.description: The closing sections revisit relaxing more fully, sinking into the earth-body, and resting in the vast awake silence that remains aware.

venue: Insight Meditation Community of Washington DC

title: Earth's Crisis - On the Edge of the Roof - (on climate change)
speaker: Tara Brach
description: (Insight Meditation Community of Washington DC) This talk views the ecological dis-ease of our planet through the lens of our evolutionary unfolding. We explore the egoic trance that has precipitated the destruction of our environment, and the inner practices of presence that enable us to respond from love and wisdom.
podcast_description: Tara Brach explores climate crisis through Dharma, using Rumi, the Parsifal legend, and Joanna Macy to show how denial, grasping, and disconnection keep both people and the planet in a kind of trance. She moves from the scale of ecological loss to the inner work of feeling, presence, and belonging, then toward the question of what right action looks like without certainty about outcomes. The talk closes with a prayerful invitation to listen more deeply to the living earth and respond from care.
short_summary: A Dharma talk on climate crisis, trance, grief, and the practice of responding from belonging rather than fear.

chapters.title: Opening the crisis
chapters.description: Brach frames the climate emergency as both a planetary event and a spiritual challenge, linking ecological harm to the habits of indifference and waking up.

chapters.title: Rumi and Parsifal
chapters.description: Rumi's warning about being drunk at the edge of the roof becomes a bridge into the Parsifal legend, where one honest question restores life to a wasteland.

chapters.title: Trance and consumption
chapters.description: She describes the egoic trance as grasping, numbness, and objectifying the world, drawing on the Sutra on the Son's Flesh and the logic of endless consumption.

chapters.title: Separation and entitlement
chapters.description: The talk expands into a developmental view of separation, fear, and entitlement, including cultural patterns of growth, extraction, and a sense that humans stand apart from the earth.

chapters.title: Digital distance
chapters.description: A contemporary texting story leads into a reflection on how cyber-life can flatten depth, embodiment, and compassion, even while increasing information.

chapters.title: Earth as belonging
chapters.description: Through Joanna Macy and Chief Seattle, Brach emphasizes that the earth is not a supply source but a shared body, and that shutting down in fear is a central danger.

chapters.title: Feeling and action
chapters.description: She shifts from diagnosis to practice: deepening attention, feeling what is here, not relying on hope of results, and asking what the earth requires of us now.

chapters.title: Collective awakening
chapters.description: Brach points toward systemic change, community, and teachings from Paul Hawken, Darwin, and Emerson that reframe life as interdependent, urgent, and worthy of wonder.

chapters.title: Listening and prayer
chapters.description: The closing turns into a brief contemplative appeal to hear the animals, plants, and planet itself, and to let prayer become response.

venue: Insight Meditation Community of Washington DC

title: Buddhist Perspective On Climate Change
speaker: Sayadaw Vivekananda
description: (Insight Meditation Society - Forest Refuge) Observed changes in the climate system, attribution of climate change, Buddhist perspective, Buddhist response to climate change.

title: Practice & Climate Change
speaker: Thanissara
description: (Insight Meditation Society - Retreat Center) Knowing your refuge. Cultivating resilience. Alignment with authentic response.

title: Wise Action for the Earth
speaker: David Loy
description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center)

title: Right Action on Climate
speaker: Ayya Anandabodhi
description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center)

title: Today’s Climate Reality
speaker: Ayya Santussika
description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center)

title: How To Be an Earthling
speaker: Wes Nisker
description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center) with Kevin Griffin on guitar

title: Compassion As We Take Action
speaker: Jack Kornfield
description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center) Recorded from Spirit Rock's Earth Day Gathering

title: The Eco-Crisis as a Spiritual Crisis
speaker: David Loy
description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center) There are precise and profound parallels between what Buddhism says about our usual individual predicament and our collective ecological predicament today.

title: Bob Doppelt, James Baraz: Earth As Witness: International Dharma Teachers' Statement on Climate Change
speaker: James Baraz
description: (Insight Meditation Community of Berkeley)

title: The Theatre of Selves (Part Three)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)

title: Climate Change, Capitalism, and Buddhadharma
speaker: Joanna Macy
description: (Insight Meditation Community of Berkeley) An impassioned talk that includes a little known story from the suttas about the dangers of and suffering caused by the notion of private property. During the talk, winds of 45 mph buffeted the monastery, emergency sirens sounded in the distance, and three power outages occurred. Ends with a lively discussion with the sangha.

title: The Theatre of Selves (Part Two)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)

title: The Theatre of Selves (Part One)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)

title: Wise Responses to the Hindrances
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)

title: All In This Together
speaker: Catherine McGee
description: (Gaia House) The inter-relationship between inner and outer. Knowing that we belong as a basis for meeting and responding to the suffering of our world, both inner and outer.

title: The Anatomy of Ignorance 2 - The Social Roots of Ignorance and climate Disruption
speaker: Donald Rothberg
description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center) For Earth Care Week we review the three aspects of ignorance examined last week - personal (or psychological), social, and universal. We focus on the social roots of ignorance with attention mostly to understanding and responding to the climate crisis.

title: The Broader Context of Mindfulness
speaker: Mark Coleman
description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center) Mindfulness is so much more that attention. Supporting wholesome qualities like patience and equanimity, Right Mindfulness specifically develops wise action in our lives and in the world.

title: Practicing in Times of Big Changes
speaker: Ayya Santacitta
description: (Aloka Vihara Forest Monastery (Hartford Street Zen Center))
venue: Aloka Vihara Forest Monastery (Hartford Street Zen Center

title: Follow Your Heartbreak
speaker: James Baraz
description: (Insight Meditation Community of Berkeley) Inspired by Andrew Harvey's book A Guide to Sacred Activism. The journey of transformation, both personal and global, includes our hearts opening to all the suffering we encounter and letting our heart be shattered and break open to new possibilities. This is a natural and essential component of true awakening. This process is explored on different levels: our own dharma practice, the classical Progress of Insight and what Harvey calls "The Dark Night of the Species."

title: The Dharma Of Global Climate Change
speaker: Chas DiCapua
description: (Insight Meditation Society - Retreat Center) How our dharma practice can turn this difficult situation onto an opportunity for practice and awakening.

speaker: Ayya Santacitta

speaker: Joanna Macy

title: Connecting Inner and Outer Responses to Climate Change
speaker: Donald Rothberg
description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center) At this time of climate disruption, we need powerful responses--integrating more "inner" spiritual practices and principles, on the one hand, with skill in "outer" responses, on the other. This integration or marriage can happen in many ways as we participate in the "great turning"--whether our primary emphasis, to use Joanna Macy's analysis, is stopping further damage from occurring, transforming our institutions, or helping to shift consciousness. Without this integration, however, spiritual practice runs the risk of becoming a kind of middle-class escapism and activism runs the risk of being caught in self-righteousness, attachment to views, demonization of the "enemy," and burnout. We need a new integration! We look at several dharma principles that can be the basis for such an integration, consider briefly how Spirit Rock is responding (and might respond further) to climate issues, and especially look at the figure of the bodhisattva.

title: Bob Doppelt, James Baraz: From Me to We
speaker: James Baraz
description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center)

title: Dharma practice and the climate Crisis
speaker: Donald Rothberg
description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center) At the time of Earth Day, we pose a a dilemma and challenge. Dharma practice is so compelling. The climate crisis is so compelling. Which do we choose? Both, in our own ways. We look at the ways that our practice (and principles of generosity, compassion, ethics, interdependence, and wisdom) call us to regard and that those responding deeply need spiritual practices and principles to meet the great need.

speaker: James Baraz
description:
(Insight Meditation Community of Berkeley) This is part 4 of the series 'Appropriate Response at the Tipping Point' that began 6/14. In this talk, James discusses the last two of the five transformational commitments in Bob Doppelt's book From Me to We:

4. Acknowledge your trustee obligations and take responsibility for the continuation of all life
5. Choose your own destiny

title: Karma and Sila as Gaia Practices
speaker: James Baraz
description:
(Insight Meditation Community of Berkeley) This is part 3 of the series 'Appropriate Response at the Tipping Point' that began 6/14. In this talk, James discusses the first three of the five transformational commitments in Bob Doppelt's book From Me to We:

1. See the systems you are part of
2. Be accountable for all the consequences of your actions
3. Abide by society’s most deeply held universal principles of morality and justice

title: Seeing the System You Are Part Of
speaker: James Baraz
description: (Insight Meditation Community of Berkeley) Sharing a positive vision for the future from Ernest Callenbach’s "Epistle to Ecotopians". We then begin to explore the first of five Dharma principles using systems and sustainability expert Bob Doppelt’s book From Me to We. The ignorance of these principles is what perpetuates the problem and the understanding of them is the key to changing our consciousness and providing a path toward healing the planet. This is part 2 of the series 'Appropriate Response at the Tipping Point' that began June 14.

title: Appropriate Response at the Tipping Point: Concern for the Planet as Dharma Practice
speaker: James Baraz
description: (Insight Meditation Community of Berkeley) This is the first of a four-part series inspired by the Spring 2012 issue of Inquiring Mind, entitled 'Earth Now.' In addition to reading this issue, especially the interview with Joanna Macy: Woman on the Edge of Time, James also suggested these readings: Ernest Callenbach’s essay "Epistle to Ecotopians" and Bob Doppelt's book From Me to We: The Five Transformational Commitments Required to Rescue the Planet, Your Organization, and Your Life. The series continues on 6/28, 7/5, and 7/19.

description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center)

title: For The Sake of Life on Earth, part 1
speaker: Joanna Macy
description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center) Through silence and song, sitting practice. Dharma talks, and interactive exercises, we will affirm our capacity to take part in the healing of our world. Each of our lives is played against a backdrop of war-making, hunger, mass extinctions and increasing climate chaos. The Buddha's teachings show how we can respond to the suffering of our time in ways that bring courage, gladness, and deep community. Our day together will draw on the Work That Reconnects, developed by Joanna over the last 35 years, and on Jennifer's soul-expanding music, that has empowered activists the world over. Joanna Macy is known worldwide as a Gaian teacher whose trainings strengthen movements for global justice. Her books include Dharma and Development, Coming Back To Life, Mutual Causality in Buddhist Teachings and Systems Theory, and her memoir Widening Circles, as well as translations of Rilke's poetry. Jennifer Berezan is a renowned singer/songwriter whose work is informed by Buddhist and indigenous teachings as well as her adventures at the forefront of social change. Her concerts and recordings include Praises for the World and her most recent release In These Arms, a Song for All Beings, based on practices of loving kindness and compassion.

title: Healing Ecology - A Buddhist Perspective on the Eco Crisis
speaker: David Loy
description: (Gaia House) There are precise and profound parallels between what Buddhism says about our individual predicament and our collective predicament today in relationship to the rest of the biosphere.

title: Realizing the Nature of Mind
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Insight Meditation Society - Forest Refuge) Through practice we can glimpse a sense of the nature of awareness as something ever present and awesomely vast, and this sense can be cultivated as a profound resource for freedom and peace in our lives. But eventually we must see even beyond this to know the ultimate nature of the mind - empty, completely groundless, and dependently-arisen - a seeing which brings an even deeper freedom. This talk explores some of the ways this realization might be encouraged and developed in meditation.

title: No Self, No World
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Insight Meditation Society - Forest Refuge) Anatta (‘not-self’, or ‘no-self’) is one of the Buddha’s most subtle and profound teachings, and a full understanding of it involves seeing how not only the personal self, but also the entire world of experience, is empty of any intrinsic essence or existence. This talk explores some of the possible ways a meditator might work in practice to develop and strengthen such radical and liberating insights.

title: Into the depths of Silence
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Insight Meditation Society - Forest Refuge) Listening to silence in our lives, opening to its embrace, reveals a profound and immense power to transform the heart. Deepening in the stillness of meditation, our practice involves mindfulness of all ‘objects’, but must eventually also go beyond objects to realize a truly boundless freedom – of being nothing and having nothing.

title: What is ‘Insight’?
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Insight Meditation Society - Forest Refuge) This talk presents a map and overview of the path of Insight Meditation, exploring the different kinds and levels of insight available to us, the possible avenues for its development and deepening, and some of the many ways we can nurture and strengthen its unfolding.

description: (Gaia House) Please note that these talks are from a 4 week retreat for experienced meditators. The talks and meditations can be listened to in any order or individually, but as they progressively unfold different levels of understanding of Emptiness, they will probably be more fully understood and the practices more easily developed if taken in series

title: Beyond Impermanence
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)

title: The Meaning of Refuge
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)

title: Maya and Nirvana (Beyond the Measure of Mind)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) What did the Buddha mean when he said that the world is 'like an illusion or a magic show' and that 'only nirvana is real'? This talk explores some of the Buddha's teaching of Dependent Arising, and how, through right practice, the illusion may be untangled, revealing the true nature of things, of mind, and of Nirvana.

title: Living in the Truth
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) A life dedicated to discovering the Truth of things is a life lived beautifully, and leading organically to freedom from suffering. Yet we frequently approach our seeking with hidden attachments to assumptions, preconceptions and views (often about Truth or the ways it is realised) that hinder a really complete, far-reaching, open and radical inquiry. On every level, from the personal to the mystical and ultimate, how can we give free reign to the heart's longing to live the truth?

title: Sense Desire
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) We exist and move in the world of sense contacts, and yet often we neglect to examine this relationship very deeply, or it becomes just another way to judge ourselves. Can we challenge our assumptions, habits and views and inquire caringly in this area in order to open to a more profound and unexpected freedom?

title: Working with Thought in Meditation
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) As meditators, it can be tempting to see the thinking mind as an enemy, but we can instead discover a wise and skillful relationship with it. This talk presents possible ways of working with the wide range of manifestations of thought: from papanca (proliferation) and obsession to creative, reflective and helpful thinking, to seeing the empty nature of all thought.

title: Approaches to the Hindrances

title: Talk Five: Samatha, Nibbana, and the Emptiness of Perception - The Relationship Between Concentration and Insight
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) As we learn to develop concentration in meditation, samatha (calm, tranquility) is also developed, and together these qualities become a powerful means for deep insight and a source of profound well-being. This progressive series of talks, guided meditations and instructions explores in some detail the art of concentration, primarily through different ways of working with the breath and the body to open to deeper and deeper levels of calmness, presence and joy.

title: Talk One: Introduction to the Art of Concentration (Samatha Meditation)
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) As we learn to develop concentration in meditation, samatha (calm, tranquility) is also developed, and together these qualities become a powerful means for deep insight and a source of profound well-being. This progressive series of talks, guided meditations and instructions explores in some detail the art of concentration, primarily through different ways of working with the breath and the body to open to deeper and deeper levels of calmness, presence and joy.

title: Nirvana and Transcendence
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Is this it? Or has transcendence gone out of fashion? An exploration of the Buddha's radical teachings on transcendence and their implications for our practices and for our lives.

title: Awakening
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) The Realization of Awakening is absolutely central to the Buddha's Teaching, and yet it has come to have so many different meanings, or none at all. What is our relationship to the idea of Enlightenment or Awakening? How does that affect our life and practice? Can we explore the views, assumptions and emotions surrounding it? And is a radical Awakening possible for us?

title: Love/The Difficulties of Love
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) This talk begins to explore some of the challenges and difficulties we can sometimes encounter with regard to love. It also examines some possible beliefs and attitudes we may hold around love and particularly romantic love in our lives.

title: Death
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) A free and honest life includes the very real awareness of death. If we can find the courage to deliberately contemplate death, to keep it in mind, this can open our life in a profound way to a nobility, urgency, purposefulness and beauty. The heart grows in compassion and moves toward the Deathless.

title: Opening Talk for Work Retreat - Working and Awakening
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Opening talk for a retreat that looks at how we may create artificial divisions between what seems 'sacred' and what seems 'worldly'; between 'retreat life' and 'everyday life', 'meditation' and work' and so seek transformation through only a small part of our lives. In opening to a profound wisdom that sees beyond such dualities, we can come to know an authentic freedom that is truly boundless.

title: The Birth of a Bodhisattva
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) This series of talks and guided meditations explores the development of the practices of both Lovingkindness and Compassion, with particular emphasis on the radical possibilities of Awakening that they bring. Through these practices we come to develop deep and beautiful qualities of heart as a real resource both for ourselves and the world, and also open ourselves to the profound and liberating understandings that can emerge from this path of love.

title: Love and the Emptiness of Things
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) This series of talks and guided meditations explores the development of the practices of both Lovingkindness and Compassion, with particular emphasis on the radical possibilities of Awakening that they bring. title: The Place of Samadhi in Metta Practice
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) This series of talks and guided meditations explores the development of the practices of both Lovingkindness and Compassion, with particular emphasis on the radical possibilities of Awakening that they bring. Through these practices we come to develop deep and beautiful qualities of heart as a real resource both for ourselves and the world, and also open ourselves to the profound and liberating understandings that can emerge from this path of love.

title: Joy
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) The Buddha placed great emphasis on joy (mudita) and Happiness (sukkha) as part of the path. This talk explores the place of these beautiful and precious qualities in our practice, and life. What are the foundations of happiness? What is it that allows the heart to open to joy?

title: What is Insight?
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) This talk presents a map and overview of the path of Insight Meditation, exploring the different kinds and levels of insight available to us, the possible avenues for its development and deepening, and some of the many ways we can nurture and strengthen its unfolding.

title: Time and the Emptiness of Time
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Time is one of the fundamental aspects of existence we usually take for granted, leaving its seeming ‘reality’ unquestioned. But Dharma practice and inquiry can puncture and shatter our belief in the real independent existence of time – both the past/future and the present/the ‘Now’, and, in so doing, open awareness up to a truly inconceivable and immeasurable liberation.

title: Into the Depths of Silence
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House)

title: A Question of Faith
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) A probing exploration of the movements of faith in our practice. Whether we are conscious of it or not, we are always placing our faith somewhere. What do we have faith in? What shapes our sense of limitation or possibility? And how do faith and confidence deepen?

title: Freedom From Fear And Anxiety
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Insight Meditation Society - Retreat Center) This talk explores in detail the tools and approaches we can develop to work skillfully with fear in our lives, both those fears that are obvious and those that are more hidden. There is a genuine possibility of liberation from the power that fear seems to have to constrict us

title: Passion, Desire, and the Path
speaker: Rob Burbea
description: (Gaia House) Often in spiritual traditions desire is seen simply as something to rid ourselves of, but there is a very important place for it on the path. We need passion. And we need both to understand desire and to know freedom from desire.
